Overview & Primary Legislation
The UK fire safety legislative landscape is anchored by the Regulatory Reform (Fire Safety) Order 2005, supported by the Fire Safety Act 2021, Fire Safety (England) Regulations 2022, Building Safety Act 2022, and a broad framework of British Standards, approved documents, and NFCC guidance.
Primary Legislation — England & Wales · SI 2005/1541
Regulatory Reform (Fire Safety) Order 2005
As amended by the Fire Safety Act 2021 · Fire Safety (England) Regulations 2022 · Building Safety Act 2022
The RRFSO 2005 is the principal fire safety law for non-domestic premises in England and Wales. It places a duty on the Responsible Person to carry out a suitable and sufficient fire risk assessment, implement appropriate fire safety measures, and keep those measures under review. It covers virtually all workplaces, common parts of multi-occupied residential buildings, and other non-domestic premises.
Art. 9 — FRA Duty Art. 10 — Principles of Prevention Art. 11 — Fire-Fighting Measures Art. 13 — Detection & Warning Arts. 14–15 — Escape Routes Art. 21 — Training Art. 22 — Co-operation Arts. 8–23 — All Duties
RRFSO 2005 — Full Legislation Text
🟢
2026 Updates: Residential Evacuation Plans Regulations 2025 now in force. BS 8214:2026 (Fire Doors) published — supersedes BS 8214:2016. Res. Evac. Plans Regs 2025 commenced 6 January 2025.
2005
RRFSO enacted
Art. 9
Core FRA duty
Arts. 14–15
Escape routes & procedures
11m+
Buildings affected (est.)
2022
Last major amendment
Key Amending Legislation
Legislation
Fire Safety Act 2021
Clarified the scope of the RRFSO to explicitly include the structure, external walls (including cladding and balconies), and flat entrance doors of multi-occupied residential buildings.
Multi-Residential Full Act text Gov.uk Guidance
External Walls
Fire Doors
Regulation
Fire Safety (England) Regulations 2022
Imposed additional requirements on responsible persons in multi-occupied residential buildings: quarterly lift checks, monthly fire door inspections for buildings over 11m, annual flat entrance door checks, and resident information provisions.
Residential >2 Storeys Full Regs text Gov.uk Guidance
Fire Doors
High-Rise
Legislation
Building Safety Act 2022
Established the Building Safety Regulator (BSR) and the higher-risk building (HRB) regime for buildings over 18m or 7 storeys with residential use. Introduced the accountable person, principal accountable person, and safety case requirements.
HRBs — England Full Act text Gov.uk Guidance HSE / BSR Hub
Higher-Risk Regime Building Regs
Legislation
Fire & Rescue Services Act 2004
Establishes the duties and powers of fire and rescue authorities in England and Wales, including enforcement powers under the RRFSO and community fire safety functions.
England & Wales Full Act text
Key Guidance Bodies & Sources
NFCC Guidance
National Fire Chiefs Council
Publishes enforcement guidance, competency frameworks, and Fire Safety in Specialised Housing guidance (formerly Housing Act guidance). The NFCC Protection Committee produces guidance referenced by fire authorities.
NFCC Guidance Library
Gov Guidance
HSE / DLUHC / MHCLG
Department for Levelling Up, Housing and Communities (now MHCLG) publishes fire safety guides, Approved Documents, and guidance notes for responsible persons.
Gov.uk — All Fire Safety Guides (free)
LGA Guidance
Local Government Association
Produces fire safety guides for 12 premises types under the RRFSO, LGA Fire Safety in Purpose-Built Flats guide, and guidance for local authority buildings.
LGA Fire Safety
Fire Risk Assessment
The duty to carry out a suitable and sufficient fire risk assessment is the cornerstone of UK fire safety law. Article 9 RRFSO 2005 places this on the Responsible Person for all non-domestic premises and common parts of multi-occupied residential buildings.
Primary Legislation & Duties
Legislation
RRFSO Art. 9 — Duty to Carry Out FRA
The Responsible Person must make a suitable and sufficient assessment of the risks to which relevant persons are exposed, to identify the general fire precautions needed. Must be reviewed regularly and when significant changes occur. If 5 or more employees, the significant findings and any group of persons identified as especially at risk must be recorded.
All Non-Domestic Art. 9 full text Gov.uk RRFSO Guide
Art. 14 — Escape
Art. 13 — Detection
Legislation
RRFSO Art. 10 — Principles of Prevention
Where the RP implements preventive and protective measures they must follow the general principles of prevention in Schedule 1: avoiding risks, evaluating unavoidable risks, combating risks at source, adapting to technical progress, and giving priority to protective measures that protect the whole workplace.
All Non-Domestic Schedule 1 full text
Legislation
RRFSO Art. 11 — Fire-Fighting Measures
The RP must provide appropriate fire-fighting equipment, fire detectors and alarms, ensure non-automatic fire-fighting equipment is easily accessible, simple to use, and indicated by signs, and nominate competent persons to implement fire-fighting measures.
All Non-Domestic Art. 11 full text
Legislation
RRFSO Art. 18 — Competent Persons
The RP must appoint one or more competent persons to assist in undertaking preventive and protective measures. A person is competent where they have sufficient training and experience or knowledge and other qualities to enable them to assist.
All Non-Domestic Art. 18 full text
Competency Frameworks
Guidance — Premises Types
BSI PAS
PAS 79-1:2020 — Non-Domestic Premises
Publicly Available Specification for fire risk assessments in non-domestic premises. Sets out guidance on the process, content, and format of FRAs and FRA reports, including what should be recorded and how findings should be communicated.
Non-Domestic BSI (purchase) IFSM Guidance BAFE SP205
BSI PAS
PAS 79-2:2020 — Housing
Equivalent PAS for fire risk assessments in housing — specifically multi-occupied residential buildings. Addresses FRA methodology, report format, and competency requirements for the assessor in a housing context.
Residential BSI (purchase) BAFE SP205
NFCC / DLUHC
FRA Guides — 12 Premises Types
The government-endorsed guides covering: offices & shops, factories, sleeping accommodation, residential care, educational, healthcare, transport, open-air events, theatres and cinemas, open-air events, animal premises, and heritage. Available free from Gov.uk.
All Premises Types All 12 guides (free)
LGA Guidance
LGA Fire Safety in Purpose-Built Flats
Guidance note for responsible persons of purpose-built blocks of flats covering the common parts FRA, flat entrance doors, compartmentation, and the interplay between RRFSO and Housing Act 2004.
Residential Blocks LGA Fire Safety
Competency
BAFE SP205 — Third-Party FRA Certification
UKAS-accredited third-party certification scheme for fire risk assessment service providers. Provides assurance that assessors meet defined competency standards. Increasingly expected by enforcement authorities and insurers.
FRA Organisations BAFE SP205
NFCC Guidance
NFCC Enforcement Guidance — FRA Quality
NFCC guidance for fire authorities on assessing the quality and adequacy of fire risk assessments during inspection. Sets expectations for FRA content and standards fire inspectors will apply.
Enforcement NFCC Guidance (free)
Higher-Risk & High-Rise
The Building Safety Act 2022 established a dedicated regime for Higher-Risk Buildings (HRBs) — residential buildings in England over 18 metres or 7 storeys. HRBs require registration, a safety case, and are regulated by the Building Safety Regulator.
🔴
Key threshold: Higher-Risk Building = at least 18m or 7 storeys AND contains at least 2 residential units. Applies in England only under BSA 2022.
Legislation
Building Safety Act 2022
Establishes the higher-risk building (HRB) regime: mandatory registration with the BSR, appointment of Accountable Person and Principal Accountable Person, preparation and maintenance of a Safety Case, and a mandatory occurrence reporting system. Also introduces the 'golden thread' of building information.
HRBs — England Full Act text Gov.uk Guidance HSE / BSR Hub
Legislation
Fire Safety Act 2021
Amended the RRFSO to clarify that the structure, external walls (including cladding, balconies, windows), and flat entrance doors of multi-occupied residential buildings are within scope of the FRA duty.
Multi-Residential Full Act text Gov.uk Guidance
Regulation
Fire Safety (England) Regulations 2022
For buildings >11m: quarterly lift checks, annual checks of fire-fighting equipment, monthly checks of fire doors in common parts, and provision of information to residents. For HRBs >18m: additional requirements including wayfinding signage and information boxes for FRS.
Multi-Residential >11m / >18m Full Regs text Gov.uk Guidance
Regulation
HRB Requirements & Regs 2023
The Higher-Risk Buildings (Key Building Information etc.) Regulations 2023 set out the information that must be held in the safety case and provided to the Building Safety Regulator, including fire safety systems, structure, and occupation information.
HRBs — England Full Regs text HSE HRB Guidance
Regulation 2025
Residential Evacuation Plans Regulations 2025
Commenced 6 January 2025. Requires responsible persons of certain multi-occupied residential buildings to prepare, maintain, and keep under review a personal emergency evacuation plan (PEEP) process for residents who may need assistance evacuating in the event of fire.
Multi-Residential Full Regs text Gov.uk Guidance
BSR Guidance
Building Safety Case & Report
The HSE/BSR guidance on the safety case framework sets out what the Accountable Person must do to demonstrate that the building is safe. The safety case is a living document showing ongoing management of structural and fire risks.
HRBs HSE Safety Case Guidance BSR Hub
DLUHC Guidance
Guidance on the Building Safety Act 2022
Government guidance on the new building safety regime: registration, accountable persons, safety cases, the new homes ombudsman, and leaseholder protections regarding building safety remediation costs.
HRBs — England Gov.uk BSA Guidance HSE / BSR
Independent Review
Dame Judith Hackitt Review 2018
Building a Safer Future — independent review commissioned after the Grenfell Tower fire. Identified systemic failures in the building safety regulatory system and set out the framework that became the Building Safety Act 2022.
Policy & Reform Full report (free PDF)
Passive Fire Protection
Passive fire protection encompasses compartmentation, fire-resisting construction, fire doors, cavity barriers, and intumescent products — all designed to slow or prevent fire and smoke spread without active intervention. Governed by BS standards, Approved Document B, and the RRFSO.
Legislation & Regulations
Legislation
RRFSO 2005 — Compartmentation Duty
Article 9 requires the RP to assess risks from fire spread. Schedule 1 General Principles of Prevention includes combating risks at source — maintaining fire-resisting compartmentation is a key preventive measure. Article 17 requires maintenance of fire precautions including structural measures.
All Non-Domestic Art. 17 full text
Legislation
Building Regulations 2010 — Regulation 38
Regulation 38 requires that fire safety information is given to the responsible person upon completion of building works, including information about the passive fire protection measures installed. This supports the 'golden thread' concept and ongoing maintenance.
New Buildings & Refurb Reg. 38 full text AD B (free)
Legislation
Construction Products Regulation (CPR)
Retained EU regulation (as amended) requiring construction products, including fire doors and passive fire protection products, to have a Declaration of Performance (DoP) and CE or UKCA marking where covered by a harmonised standard (EN). Critical for product compliance verification in FRAs.
All Construction Products Gov.uk CPR Guidance
Regulation
Fire Safety (England) Regulations 2022 — Fire Door Checks
Reg. 10: Monthly checks of fire doors in common parts of buildings over 11m. Annual checks of flat entrance doors that are fire doors. Responsible persons must keep records of checks and act on defects promptly. Checks must confirm self-closing devices work, gaps are within limits, and seals are intact.
Multi-Residential >11m Full Regs text Gov.uk Guidance
Fire Doors — Standards & Guidance
British Standard 2026
BS 8214:2026 — Fire Door Assemblies
Current edition (supersedes BS 8214:2016). Specification for fire door assemblies — including frames, ironmongery, glazing, and seals. Sets out design, installation, and maintenance requirements. FD30 and FD60 classifications. Essential reference for all fire door assessments.
All Buildings BSI (purchase) FDIS Free Guides BWF Free Guidance
Compartmentation
Approved Doc B
British Standard
BS 8214:2016 — Fire Door Assemblies (Superseded)
Previous edition, superseded by BS 8214:2026 but still referenced in legacy installations. Door sets tested and certified to this standard prior to 2026 remain valid provided they maintain their certification and are maintained in accordance with the manufacturer's instructions.
Legacy BSI (purchase) FDIS Free Guides BWF Free Guidance
Guidance
FDIS — Fire Door Inspection Scheme
Guild of Architectural Ironmongery / BWF scheme for inspecting fire doors in existing buildings. Qualified FDIS inspectors assess fire door condition against the installed specification and current standards, producing detailed inspection reports.
Inspection & Maintenance FDIS.co.uk Inspection Guidance (free)
BWF Guidance
BWF Fire Door Alliance
The British Woodworking Federation Fire Door Alliance provides guidance on fire door specification, installation, inspection, and maintenance. Guidance documents and training available, including the 'Five Step Fire Door Check' for responsible persons.
All Buildings BWF Fire Door Guidance (free) BWF FireSafe
British Standard
BS EN 1634-1 — Fire Resistance Tests for Doors
European standard for fire resistance and smoke control tests for door and shutter assemblies and openable windows. The test standard against which fire door sets achieve their E (integrity) and EI (integrity + insulation) classifications.
Product Testing BSI Standards (purchase) ASFP Free Guidance
Guidance
BS 476 Parts 20–23 — Legacy Fire Tests
Legacy British Standard fire test series (replaced by EN standards for most applications). Part 22 covers fire resistance of non-loadbearing elements. Many existing certified products were tested to BS 476 and their certification remains valid. Understanding legacy certification is essential for FRAs of older stock.
Legacy Product Certification BSI Standards (purchase) ASFP Free Guidance
Compartmentation & Structural Fire Protection
British Standard
BS 9999:2017 — Fire Safety in the Design, Management & Use of Buildings
Comprehensive standard for fire safety in buildings. Covers design including compartmentation, escape, detection and alarm, fire-fighting facilities, and management. Used as an alternative to Approved Document B for complex or atypical buildings. Includes population profiles, occupancy factor tables, and travel distance guidance.
Design & Management BSI (purchase) AD B (free alternative)
Travel Distances
Approved Doc B
British Standard
BS 9991:2015+A1:2021 — Residential Buildings
Fire safety in the design, management, and use of residential buildings — the residential counterpart to BS 9999. Covers protected escape routes, compartmentation, sprinkler provisions, evacuation strategy, and detection requirements for blocks of flats, student accommodation, and sheltered housing.
Residential Buildings BSI (purchase) AD B Vol.1 (free)
ASFP Guidance
Association for Specialist Fire Protection (ASFP)
ASFP produces technical guidance on passive fire protection installation: fire stopping, penetration seals, cavity barriers, intumescent coatings, and fire-resistant glazing. The ASFP Yellow Book, Red Book, and Blue Book are key industry references. NEW 2026: ASFP Guide to Inspecting PFP for Fire Risk Assessors — 2nd Edition now in your Library (doc022).
Specialist Contractors ASFP Technical Guidance (some free)
British Standard
BS EN 13501-2 — Fire Classification of Products
Classifies construction products and building elements by their fire resistance performance: R (load bearing), E (integrity), I (insulation). Used in conjunction with Approved Document B to specify the correct class of fire-resisting construction (e.g. REI 60 for a 60-minute floor).
Product & Element Classification BSI Standards (purchase) ASFP Free Guidance
Detection, Warning & Suppression
Fire detection, warning, and suppression systems are covered by Article 13 RRFSO 2005 and a comprehensive suite of British Standards. The choice and design of system depends on the risk profile, building use, occupancy, and whether simultaneous or phased evacuation is adopted.
Primary Legislation
Legislation
RRFSO Art. 13 — Detection, Warning & Fire-Fighting
Requires that appropriate fire detection and warning systems are provided and maintained in an efficient state. The type of system appropriate depends on the risk assessment findings. For complex buildings with phased evacuation, an L1 automatic detection system with two-stage alarm may be required.
All Non-Domestic Source
Fire Detection & Alarm Standards
British Standard
BS 5839-1:2017 — Fire Detection & Alarm — Non-Domestic
The principal standard for fire detection and alarm systems in non-domestic premises. Defines system categories: M (manual), L1–L5 (life protection), P1–P2 (property protection). Sets out design, installation, commissioning, maintenance, and testing requirements. L1 provides automatic detection throughout; L5 protects only the means of escape.
Non-Domestic BSI (purchase) FIA Free Guidance Notes BAFE SP203
Evacuation Strategy
NFCC Guidance
British Standard
BS 5839-6:2019 — Domestic Premises
Fire detection and alarm systems in domestic premises. Grades A–F describe the type of system from full BS 5839-1 panel (Grade A) through to battery-powered detectors (Grade F). Categories LD1–LD3 describe detection coverage. Grade D/LD2 interlinked mains-powered smoke detectors is now widely required in existing dwellings. LD1 provides detection throughout.
Domestic Premises BSI (purchase) FIA Free Guidance Notes BAFE SP203
British Standard
BS EN 54 Series — Fire Detection Components
European series covering individual fire detection and fire alarm system components: control panels (Part 2), point smoke detectors (Part 7), point heat detectors (Part 5), multi-sensor detectors (Part 29/30), beam detectors (Part 12), and many more. Components must conform to EN 54 for CE/UKCA marking.
Product Compliance BSI Standards (purchase) ASFP Free Guidance
British Standard
BS 5588 Series (Legacy)
Legacy fire precautions in design, construction, and use of buildings. Now largely superseded by BS 9999 and BS 9991, but Part 11 (offices) and other parts may be referenced in older FRA findings. Understanding the legacy standards is important when assessing buildings designed and built to them.
Legacy BSI Standards (purchase) ASFP Free Guidance
Suppression Systems
British Standard
BS EN 12845 — Fixed Firefighting — Sprinklers
Design, installation, and maintenance of fixed automatic sprinkler systems. European standard with UK national application document. Hazard class categories (OH, EH1–3, HH1–3) determine sprinkler density and coverage area. Essential for warehouses, hotels, and increasingly required in HRBs and schools.
Commercial & Residential BSI Standards (purchase) ASFP Free Guidance
British Standard
BS 9251:2021 — Sprinklers for Residential Premises
Residential and domestic sprinkler systems — design, installation, maintenance, and testing. Specific to homes, flats, and care homes. Residential sprinkler heads are concealed and operate at lower temperatures. Required by AD B for new houses of 3+ storeys (Wales mandates in all new dwellings).
Residential BSI (purchase) BAFSA Free Guidance AD B Vol.1 (free)
British Standard
BS 9252:2022 — Residential Sprinklers (NI/Scotland)
Residential sprinkler systems — guidance for Northern Ireland and Scotland (where devolved housing regulations differ). Design and installation for all new dwellings where mandated by devolved building regulations.
NI & Scotland BSI Standards (purchase) ASFP Free Guidance
British Standard
BS 5306 — Fire Extinguishing Equipment
Series covering portable fire extinguishers and other first aid fire-fighting equipment. Part 3: inspection and maintenance of portable extinguishers. Part 8: selection and installation. Covers CO2, dry powder, foam, water, and wet chemical extinguishers. Extinguisher selection must be appropriate for fire classification (A, B, C, F).
All Buildings BSI Standards (purchase) ASFP Free Guidance
British Standard
BS 5839-9 — Emergency Voice Communication
Design, installation, commissioning, and maintenance of emergency voice communication systems (EVCS) in buildings. Used to facilitate communication between floor wardens and a central control point, particularly important in phased evacuation high-rise buildings.
High-Rise / Complex BSI Standards (purchase) ASFP Free Guidance
Evacuation & Escape
Articles 14 and 15 of the RRFSO 2005 require the Responsible Person to provide and maintain appropriate routes and procedures for emergency evacuation. Key metrics including travel distances, door widths, and stair capacities are set out in Approved Document B and BS 9999.
Primary Legislation
Legislation
RRFSO Art. 14 — Emergency Routes & Exits
Routes and exits must be kept clear and, as far as reasonably practicable, lead as directly as possible to a place of safety. They must be of adequate number and size. Emergency routes must be indicated by signs and where necessary be provided with emergency lighting.
All Non-Domestic Art. 14 full text Gov.uk RRFSO Guide
Legislation
RRFSO Art. 15 — Procedures & Means of Escape
Requires appropriate procedures for serious and imminent danger and for danger areas, including nominating a sufficient number of competent persons to implement evacuation procedures. Also requires that persons at work are not prevented from taking steps to protect themselves when there is serious and imminent danger.
All Non-Domestic Art. 15 full text
Regulation 2025
Residential Evacuation Plans Regulations 2025
From 6 January 2025, responsible persons of multi-occupied residential buildings must have a process to offer and where agreed produce PEEPs for residents who cannot self-evacuate. Records must be kept and plans reviewed periodically or when circumstances change.
Multi-Residential Full Regs text Gov.uk Guidance
Key Distances & Dimensions — Quick Reference
Building / Occupancy Type Max Travel Distance (1 Direction) Max Travel Distance (2+ Directions) Source
Office (Residential Purpose Group) 18m 45m AD B Vol.2
Factory / Industrial 25m 45m AD B Vol.2
Assembly & Recreation (seated) 15m 32m AD B Vol.2
Blocks of Flats (common parts) 7.5m (dead end) / 30m 30m AD B Vol.1
Shops & Retail 18m 45m AD B Vol.2
Healthcare (sleeping risk) 9m 18m (non-sprinklered) AD B / HTM 05
Hotel / Guest House 9m (dead end) 18m AD B Vol.2
Warehouses / Storage 25m 45m AD B Vol.2
Standards & Guidance
British Standard
BS 9999:2017 — Evacuation Strategy (Non-Domestic)
Sets out the evacuation strategy framework for non-residential buildings. Covers simultaneous evacuation, phased evacuation, progressive horizontal evacuation (for healthcare), and defend-in-place. Includes detailed guidance on travel distances by occupancy profile (population densities A–E and occupancy factors). Useful alternative to AD B for complex buildings.
Non-Domestic BSI (purchase) AD B (free alternative)
British Standard
BS 9999:2017 Management Annex
The management annex to BS 9999 covers fire safety management in occupied buildings: fire emergency plan, fire drills, warden duties, training records, and review. Closely aligned with RRFSO Art. 21 training requirements. Sets out what a fire emergency plan should contain and how drills should be conducted.
Non-Domestic BSI (purchase) AD B (free alternative)
British Standard
BS 5266-1:2016 — Emergency Lighting
Code of practice for the emergency lighting of premises — design, installation, commissioning, and maintenance. Sets out categories: escape route lighting, open area (anti-panic) lighting, and high-risk task area lighting. Minimum illumination levels and duration (1 or 3 hours). Monthly function tests and annual full-duration tests required.
All Buildings BSI (purchase) RRFSO Art.14 (emergency lighting duty)
British Standard
BS 8300:2018 — Accessibility & Fire
Design of accessible and inclusive buildings. Directly relevant to fire safety: covers refuge areas, evacuation lifts, and the design of escape routes to ensure accessibility. PEEPs and evacuation chairs must take account of the building's physical provision under BS 8300.
All Buildings BSI (purchase) AD M (free)
PEEPs
Res. Evac. Plans Regs
British Standard
BS 9997:2019 — Evacuation Strategy (Residential)
Evacuation strategy for multi-occupied residential buildings. Covers stay-put (defend-in-place), simultaneous, and temporary simultaneous evacuation. Includes triggers for reviewing a stay-put strategy and the role of the Fire Emergency Plan in communicating strategy to residents and FRS.
Residential Blocks BSI (purchase) AD B (free alternative basis)
Emergency Evacuation Drawings
Guidance
Emergency Evacuation Drawings (EEDs)
An Emergency Evacuation Drawing (EED) is a floor plan-based diagram displayed within a building showing occupants the layout of the floor, the location of escape routes, exits, assembly points, fire fighting equipment, alarm call points, and any refuges. They are distinct from fire escape plans — an EED is a visual aid for occupants, while a fire emergency plan is the RP's management document. When are EEDs required? EEDs are not explicitly mandated by RRFSO 2005 for all premises, but are strongly implied by RRFSO Art. 14 (escape routes indicated by signs) and Art. 11 (emergency procedures communicated to occupants). They are specifically required under: • FS(E) Regulations 2022 — information about evacuation strategy must be displayed in the common parts of multi-occupied residential buildings, including high-rise buildings where floor-level evacuation information must be provided • BS 9999:2017 Annex D — recommends fire action notices and floor plans displayed at each storey • BS 9991:2015 — recommends evacuation strategy communicated to residents via signage including floor plans • HTM 05-01 — EEDs required for all healthcare premises EED content — what should be shown: • North point and floor number/level • All escape routes and final exits — with directional arrows • Assembly point location (and distance/direction if off-plan) • Location of nearest manual call point (break glass) • Fire extinguisher and hose reel locations • Refuge locations (where PHE or phased evacuation is adopted) • Lift(s) — marked "Do Not Use in Fire" • "You are here" marker • Any fire compartment boundaries relevant to the occupant • Evacuation strategy summary (e.g. "On hearing the alarm, leave immediately by the nearest exit") Format and display: • Displayed at each floor — at lift lobbies, stairwell entrances, main corridors • Minimum A3 size recommended for legibility • Must comply with BS ISO 23601:2020 (Safety identification — Evacuation and emergency signs) and BS EN ISO 7010 (safety signs) • For high-rise residential under FS(E) Regs 2022 — floor identification signage and wayfinding must be provided Who produces EEDs? Typically the FRA practitioner, facilities manager, or a specialist drawing service. They must be kept up to date — any layout change, new exit, or change of evacuation strategy requires revision. The RP has a duty under RRFSO Art. 17 (maintenance) to keep fire safety provisions, including signage and information displays, in efficient working order.
All Premises — Particularly Multi-Occupied & High-Rise RRFSO Art. 14 FS(E) Regs 2022 BS 9999:2017 Annex D
Construction & Building Regs
Approved Document B (AD B) is the primary technical guidance supporting compliance with Requirement B of the Building Regulations 2010. It covers means of warning and escape, internal fire spread (linings and structure), external fire spread, and access/facilities for the fire service.
Building Regulations & Approved Documents
Legislation
Building Regulations 2010 — Part B
The Building Regulations 2010 Schedule 1, Part B sets out the functional Requirements B1–B5: B1 (means of warning and escape), B2 (internal fire spread — linings), B3 (internal fire spread — structure), B4 (external fire spread), B5 (access and facilities for fire service). Compliance with AD B is the principal route to meeting these requirements.
England — New Build & Material Change Full Regs text All Approved Documents (free)
Approved Document
Approved Document B Vol. 1 — Dwellings (2019 + 2022 Amends)
Guidance for new dwellings including houses, flats, and maisonettes. Covers smoke alarms, escape windows, staircase configuration, compartmentation, sprinkler requirements (3+ storey houses in England), external fire spread (surface spread and cavity barriers), and fire service access. The 2022 amendment introduced sprinkler requirements for new flats in buildings over 11m.
Dwellings — England AD B Vol.1 & 2 (free PDF)
Sprinklers
Escape Distances
External Walls
Approved Document
Approved Document B Vol. 2 — Buildings Other Than Dwellings (2019 + 2022 Amends)
Guidance for all non-domestic buildings and mixed-use developments. Contains the key data tables for travel distances, exit widths, stair capacities, compartment sizes, and fire resistance periods for structural elements. The 2022 amendment introduced sprinkler requirements for warehouses >2,000m² and new hospitals. The 2019 edition removed the automatic sprinkler derogation for purpose groups.
Non-Domestic — England AD B Vol.1 & 2 (free PDF)
Approved Document
Approved Document A — Structure
Covers the structural stability requirements of buildings, including loading and structural fire resistance. Directly relevant to fire safety: buildings must be able to maintain structural integrity for a sufficient period during a fire to allow occupant evacuation. Fire resistance periods for structural elements tie in with AD B.
All Buildings — England AD A (free PDF)
Structural Fire
Approved Document
Approved Document M & K — Access & Safety
AD M (access to and use of buildings) and AD K (protection from falling, collision, and impact) support the fire escape strategy. AD M covers step-free access, refuge design and signage. AD K covers stair dimensions, guarding heights, and ramp gradients — all relevant to safe evacuation.
All Buildings — England All Approved Documents (free)
Fire Resistance Periods — Structural Elements (AD B Reference)
Building Type / Height Min. Fire Resistance (Structural) Loadbearing Walls Floors Source
Basement Storey (ground — 10m below ground) 60 min 60 min 60 min AD B
Ground to 5m / up to 2 storeys 30 min 30 min 30 min AD B
Up to 18m / 3–7 storeys 60 min 60 min 60 min AD B
Over 18m / 8+ storeys (residential) 90 min 90–120 min 90 min AD B
Basement >10m below ground 90 min 90 min 90 min AD B
Key Construction Standards
British Standard
BS 9991:2015+A1:2021 — Residential Buildings
The residential buildings standard providing design, management, and use guidance for purpose-built flats, sheltered housing, sheltered housing with care, and student accommodation. Includes sprinkler provisions, compartmentation requirements, protected stair criteria, and evacuation strategy. Used alongside or as alternative to AD B Vol.1.
Residential BSI (purchase) AD B Vol.1 (free)
British Standard
BS 9997:2019 — Fire Risk Management Systems
Requirements for fire risk management systems in organisations. A management systems standard aligned with ISO/IEC high-level structure. Covers policy, planning, implementation, performance evaluation, and improvement. Relevant to larger organisations and HRBs requiring a structured fire safety management system.
Organisations BSI Standards (purchase) ASFP Free Guidance
Fire Safety Management
Fire safety management encompasses training, procedures, fire emergency plans, drills, record-keeping, and the ongoing review of fire precautions. Articles 19–22 RRFSO 2005 and BS 9997/9999 set the framework.
Legislation
RRFSO Art. 21 — Training & Procedures
Employees must receive adequate fire safety training on first employment and on being exposed to new or increased risks. Training must include fire emergency procedures, the means of escape, fire-fighting equipment, and how to raise the alarm. Training must be repeated periodically, be appropriate to the risk, and take place during working hours.
All Non-Domestic Art. 21 full text Gov.uk RRFSO Guide
Legislation
RRFSO Art. 22 — Co-operation & Co-ordination
Where two or more responsible persons share premises, each must co-operate and co-ordinate their fire safety measures and inform each other of risks from their respective undertakings. Particularly relevant in multi-tenanted buildings and during construction works affecting occupied premises.
Multi-Tenanted / Shared Art. 22 full text
Legislation
RRFSO Art. 17 — Maintenance
All fire precautions — including detection, alarm, escape routes, emergency lighting, fire-fighting equipment, and any structural fire protection measures — must be maintained in an efficient state, in efficient working order, and in good repair.
All Non-Domestic Source
British Standard
BS 9997:2019 — Fire Risk Mgmt Systems
A structured management system standard for fire risk management within organisations. PDCA (Plan–Do–Check–Act) based. Useful for larger organisations seeking to demonstrate systematic compliance with RRFSO duties and to support their fire risk assessment programme.
Organisations BSI Standards (purchase) ASFP Free Guidance
British Standard
BS 9999:2017 Management Annex
The management annex within BS 9999 is particularly useful for practitioners: it provides practical templates for a fire emergency plan, describes how fire drills should be conducted and recorded, defines warden roles, and sets out what records must be kept. A practical day-to-day operational reference.
Non-Domestic BSI (purchase) AD B (free alternative)
NFCC Guidance
NFCC Enforcement Guidance
Guides fire & rescue authorities on consistent enforcement of the RRFSO: categorisation of findings, use of enforcement notices and prohibition notices, prosecution policy, and the competency standard expected of responsible persons and their fire risk assessors.
Enforcement NFCC Guidance (free)
External Walls & Cladding
External wall construction and cladding systems have been a major regulatory focus following the Grenfell Tower fire in 2017. The Fire Safety Act 2021 brought external walls explicitly within the RRFSO FRA duty, and BS 8414 / BR 135 set out the test and assessment framework for cladding systems.
Legislation
Fire Safety Act 2021 — External Wall Scope
Expressly brought the external walls of multi-occupied residential buildings (including cladding, insulation, fixings, balconies, and windows) within the scope of Article 9 RRFSO. Responsible persons must now assess the fire risk from external wall construction as part of their FRA.
Multi-Residential Full Act text Gov.uk Guidance
Approved Document
Approved Document B — External Fire Spread (Requirement B4)
AD B Requirement B4 limits the use of combustible materials in external walls of buildings over 18m. From 21 December 2018, new buildings over 18m must use only non-combustible (limited combustibility) materials in the external wall above 18m, subject to minor exceptions. From 2022 amendments, this threshold applies more stringently.
Buildings >18m — England AD B Vol.1 & 2 (free PDF)
British Standard
BS 8414-1 & 8414-2 — Large-Scale Fire Test for Cladding
Large-scale fire tests for external cladding systems — Part 1 (non-masonry support) and Part 2 (masonry support). A fire is applied at the base of a 9m high test rig representing the external wall construction. Results are interpreted against the criteria in BR 135. Used to evidence the fire performance of specific cladding system configurations.
Cladding Systems BSI (purchase) Gov.uk EWS Guidance (free) BRE BR 135
BRE Report
BR 135 — Fire Performance of External Thermal Insulation
BRE guidance document giving the classification criteria for the BS 8414 large-scale cladding fire test. Systems that pass BR 135 criteria can be used on buildings over 18m. EWS1 forms assess existing cladding against BR 135 criteria. Updated versions are referenced in guidance on remediation.
Cladding Systems BRE BR 135 info Gov.uk EWS Guidance (free)
PAS / Guidance
EWS1 External Wall System Form
The EWS1 form was developed by industry bodies (RICS, BSA, UK Finance) to allow responsible persons and lenders to assess the fire risk of external wall systems on multi-storey residential buildings. Completed by a qualified assessor; confirms whether the external wall meets current standards or requires remediation.
Multi-Residential — Existing Stock RICS EWS1 Guidance Gov.uk EWS1 Guide
Gov Guidance
Cladding Safety Scheme (CSS)
Government scheme to fund the replacement of unsafe cladding on eligible residential buildings 11m and above in England. Managed by Homes England. Freeholders and responsible persons of qualifying buildings can apply for funding to remediate non-ACM cladding systems that fail the BS 8414/BR 135 test criteria.
England — 11m+ Residential Cladding Safety Scheme
Guidance
MHCLG External Wall Guidance Notes
Guidance for responsible persons on how to undertake an assessment of the external wall system of their building under the Fire Safety Act 2021 and RRFSO. Includes the consolidated advice note (CAN) on Aluminium Composite Material (ACM) cladding systems.
Multi-Residential Gov.uk EWS Guidance (free)
Structural Integrity & Fire
Structural fire safety ensures buildings can maintain stability for long enough to allow evacuation and fire service operations. Governed by Building Regulations Part A and Part B, Eurocodes, and specialist BRE/SCI guidance. Critical for steel, concrete, timber, and mixed-construction buildings.
Approved Document
Approved Document A — Structure
Sets out the structural stability requirements including consideration of fire. Requirement A3 requires that buildings are designed to minimise the risk of disproportionate collapse. Structures must be designed to carry loads safely for the relevant fire resistance period (30, 60, 90, or 120 minutes depending on building height and use).
All Buildings — England AD A (free PDF)
Eurocode
Eurocode 1-2 — Actions on Structures During Fire
EN 1991-1-2 provides thermal and mechanical actions for fire design of structures. Defines nominal fire curves (standard, hydrocarbon, external fire) and advanced natural fire models (parametric, zone, CFD). Used by structural fire engineers to verify performance beyond prescriptive solutions.
Structural Fire Engineering BSI (purchase) EU Eurocode Hub (background docs free) SCI Design Guides
Eurocode
Eurocode 3-1-2 — Steel Structures: Fire Design
Design of steel structures for fire situations. Methods range from tabulated data through simplified calculation to advanced numerical methods. Critical heating rates for unprotected and protected steel members. Covers critical temperature method for simple members and the full interaction with structural system for complex frames.
Steel Structures BSI (purchase) EU Eurocode Hub (background docs free) SCI Design Guides
Eurocode
Eurocode 2-1-2 — Concrete Structures: Fire Design
Design of concrete structures for fire. Covers tabulated design, simplified calculation methods, and advanced methods. Key parameters include minimum cover to reinforcement, minimum member dimensions, and axis distance. Spalling of high-strength concrete in fire is also addressed.
Concrete Structures BSI (purchase) EU Eurocode Hub (background docs free) SCI Design Guides
Eurocode
Eurocode 5-1-2 — Timber Structures: Fire Design
Design of timber structures for fire. Charring rates for softwood and hardwood, reduced cross-section method, and residual cross-section calculation. Cross-laminated timber (CLT) charring and the contribution of mass timber to fire load are significant considerations in contemporary construction.
Timber Structures BSI (purchase) EU Eurocode Hub (background docs free) SCI Design Guides
Published Document
PD 7974 — Fire Safety Engineering
Suite of BSI published documents supporting fire safety engineering / performance-based design: PD 7974-0 (framework), PD 7974-1 (fire initiation), PD 7974-2 (fire spread), PD 7974-3 (structural response), PD 7974-4 (detection), PD 7974-5 (fire service), PD 7974-6 (evacuation), PD 7974-7 (probabilistic). Used for complex or innovative buildings.
Fire Engineers / Complex Buildings BSI (purchase) SFPE Guides
SCI / BRE Guidance
SCI P288 / BRE Structural Fire Guides
The Steel Construction Institute (SCI) and BRE produce practical design guides for structural fire engineering: intumescent coatings, board protection, spray protection, and structural stability in fire. SCI P288 covers fire protection of structural steelwork. Essential reference for passive structural fire protection specification.
Structural Fire Protection SCI Design Guides ASFP Passive Fire Guidance
Supporting & Cross-Cutting Legislation
A range of legislation beyond the RRFSO intersects with fire safety obligations. These include health & safety law, housing law, electrical safety, dangerous substances, and HMO licensing — all of which a fire risk assessor must understand and cross-reference.
Legislation
Health & Safety at Work etc. Act 1974
Primary framework for health and safety in workplaces across Great Britain. Fire safety sits within the broader H&S duty. The general duty on employers under Section 2 to ensure the health, safety and welfare of employees includes fire safety measures. HSWA 1974 and RRFSO run in parallel for workplaces.
All Workplaces — GB Full Act text HSE HSWA Guide
Legislation
Management of H&S at Work Regs 1999
Regulation 3 imposes the risk assessment duty for employers and self-employed persons — the H&S equivalent of RRFSO Art. 9. In multi-occupied workplaces, both RRFSO Art. 9 and Reg. 3 risk assessments are required. Regulation 5 requires arrangements for planning, organising, controlling, monitoring, and reviewing protective measures.
Employers — GB Full Regs text HSE Free Guide
Legislation
Dangerous Substances & Explosive Atmospheres Regs 2002 (DSEAR)
DSEAR requires assessment and control of risks from dangerous substances including flammable and explosive materials. Integrates with RRFSO: where premises have flammable liquids, gases, or explosive atmospheres, the DSEAR risk assessment and the FRA must be co-ordinated. Zoning of ATEX areas must be reflected in the FRA.
Workplaces with Flammables Full Regs text HSE DSEAR Guidance (free)
Legislation
Housing Act 2004 — HMO Licensing & HHSRS
Mandatory HMO licensing applies fire safety conditions to larger HMOs (3+ storeys, 5+ occupants). HHSRS Category 1 fire hazards are enforceable by local housing authorities. HMOs need both a licensing assessment under HA 2004 and a RRFSO FRA where there are non-domestic common parts. Overlapping enforcement responsibilities between LA and FRS must be understood.
HMOs Full Act text Gov.uk HHSRS Guide
Legislation
Electricity at Work Regulations 1989
Requires that electrical systems are constructed and maintained to prevent danger. Electrical faults are a leading cause of fires — FRAs must assess electrical safety. Fixed wiring periodic inspection and testing (BS 7671) and portable appliance testing are key fire risk reduction measures relevant to FRA findings under Art. 9 RRFSO.
All Premises Full Regs text HSE Memorandum of Guidance (free PDF)
Legislation
Licensing Act 2003
Fire safety is a mandatory condition of premises licences for alcohol sale and public entertainment. FRS is a responsible authority that can make representations on licence applications. Licence conditions must not conflict with RRFSO requirements but may add further occupancy and fire safety obligations.
Licensed Premises Full Act text Gov.uk Guidance
British Standard
BS 8519 — Fire-Resistant Cables
Selection and installation of fire-resistant power and control cables for fire safety systems. Circuit integrity requirements for fire alarm, emergency lighting, suppression control, and smoke control systems. Cables must maintain circuit integrity for the required period (typically 30 or 60 minutes) to allow fire safety systems to operate during a fire.
Fire System Infrastructure BSI (purchase) FIA Free Guidance
Published Document
PD 7974 — Fire Safety Engineering
Framework and sub-documents for quantified fire risk and engineering analysis. Allows performance-based demonstration of compliance for complex, novel, or heritage buildings where prescriptive solutions in AD B are not appropriate or achievable.
Fire Engineers / Complex Buildings BSI Standards (purchase) ASFP Free Guidance
Legislation + Guidance
Street & External Lighting — Responsibility & H&S Relevance
Adequate external lighting is a material consideration in fire risk assessments and H&S assessments — particularly for means of escape routes leading to assembly points, car parks, external stairs, and public footpaths forming part of an escape route. Responsibility depends on whether the street or path is adopted or private. Who is responsible? • Adopted highway — the local highway authority (council) under Highways Act 1980 s.97. Use FindMyStreet.co.uk (National Street Gazetteer) to identify the street authority for any postcode or USRN. • Private road / unadopted estate road — the developer, freeholder, or estate management company. • Within building curtilage — the Responsible Person (RRFSO) / property owner / managing agent. • Communal areas of a development — typically the managing agent, RTM company, or residents' management company. Relevant legislation: • Highways Act 1980 s.97 — duty on highway authority to light highways where appropriate • RRFSO Art. 14 — escape routes must be kept safe and usable, including to the place of ultimate safety; inadequate external lighting on an escape route is a finding • Workplace (Health, Safety & Welfare) Regs 1992 Reg. 8 — suitable and sufficient lighting in workplaces, including external areas under employer control • HSWA 1974 s.2 — general duty extends to external areas under employer control In practice for FRA: Where an escape route exits onto a poorly lit external path or car park not maintained by the RP, this should be recorded as a finding with the responsible authority identified. Use FindMyStreet to determine street authority. Recommend RP contacts the highway authority or managing agent to request remediation.
All Premises — External Escape Routes FindMyStreet — identify street authority Highways Act 1980 s.97 Workplace Regs 1992 Reg.8
Competency & Professional Bodies
Competency in fire safety is increasingly formalised through third-party accreditation, professional membership, and structured qualifications. The RRFSO requires that fire risk assessors are competent, and industry bodies set out what competency means in practice.
Professional Bodies
IFSM & IFE — Membership & CPD Frameworks
The Institution of Fire Safety Managers (IFSM) and the Institution of Fire Engineers (IFE) both provide graded professional membership: MIFSM / AIIFireE / MIFireE and beyond. Membership demonstrates competency and ongoing CPD. IFE qualifications (level 3–6) are internationally recognised. IFSM membership is focused on fire safety management practitioners.
Practitioners IFSM.org.uk IFSM Knowledge Hub
Competency
BWF-CERTIFIRE / Certifire Schemes
Third-party product certification schemes (Certifire by Efectis, BWF-CERTIFIRE for fire doors) that certify construction products to the relevant EN or BS standard. Products bearing Certifire or BWF-CERTIFIRE marks provide evidence of compliance — important when assessing fire doors in FRAs.
Product Certification Certifire.co.uk FDIS
Competency
BAFE SP205 — FRA Third-Party Certification
UKAS-accredited third-party certification scheme for fire risk assessment organisations. Inspects assessor competency, quality management, and the standard of FRA reports. Increasingly expected by enforcement authorities and by responsible persons as evidence of due diligence in appointment of a competent assessor.
FRA Organisations BAFE SP205
Competency
NFCC Competency Framework — Fire Safety Regulators
NFCC published a competency framework for fire safety regulators (inspecting officers) setting out the knowledge, skills, and behaviours required at each level. Used by FRSs to structure career development. Also informs the standard of competency the public and regulated entities can expect from inspecting officers.
FRS Inspectors NFCC Competency Framework (free)
Gov Guidance
Fire Safety Act 2021 Competency Guidance
Government guidance on the competency required to assess the external wall systems of multi-occupied residential buildings under the Fire Safety Act 2021 and RRFSO. Sets out the qualifications, experience, and professional registration expected of those undertaking external wall assessments.
External Wall Assessors Gov.uk FSA 2021 Guidance IFSM Competency Resources
Independent Review
Dame Judith Hackitt Review 2018
Identified systemic failures in competency across the building design, construction, and management industry and recommended establishing a mandatory competency framework. The foundation for the BSA 2022 provisions on registered building inspectors, regulated professionals, and the higher-risk building regime's competency requirements.
Policy & Reform Full Report (free PDF)
Quick Reference Tables
Essential numeric and classification data drawn from AD B, BS 9999, BS 5839-1, BS 5266-1, and BS 9991. All figures should be verified against the current edition of the relevant document before use in a formal fire risk assessment or design.
Smoke Detector Coverage (BS 5839-6 — Domestic)
Detector Type Max Floor Area Max Horizontal Distance to Wall Min Distance from Obstacles Source
Point Smoke Detector (ceiling mounted) 60m² (horizontal ceiling) / 7.5m radius 500mm from wall / corner 300mm from light fittings / beams <200mm deep BS 5839-6
Point Heat Detector 30m² per detector 5.3m radius 300mm from obstacles BS 5839-6
Point Smoke — Sloped Ceiling (>20°) Within 600mm of apex As per standard layout As above BS 5839-6
Smoke Detector Coverage (BS 5839-1 — Non-Domestic)
Detector Type Max Floor Area Max Horizontal Distance Height of Ceiling Source
Point Ionisation Smoke Detector 60m² 7.5m from any point Up to 10.5m BS 5839-1:2017
Point Optical Smoke Detector 60m² 7.5m from any point Up to 10.5m BS 5839-1:2017
Point Heat Detector (A1R class) 30m² 5.3m from any point Up to 7.5m BS 5839-1:2017
Multi-Sensor Detector 60m² (smoke function) 7.5m from any point Up to 10.5m BS 5839-1:2017
Linear Beam Detector Up to 14m beam path width Beam max 7m from side wall Clear of obstructions in beam path BS 5839-1:2017
Emergency Lighting — Key Requirements (BS 5266-1:2016)
Parameter Escape Route Lighting Open Area (Anti-Panic) Source
Minimum illuminance on centreline 1 lux (0.5 lux at floor, 2m each side) 0.5 lux BS 5266-1
Duration 1 hour (min) or 3 hours where maintenance impractical 1 hour (min) BS 5266-1
Changeover time 5 seconds (5% maintained systems: instantaneous) 5 seconds BS 5266-1
Monthly test Function test — brief switch to battery (record kept) As escape route BS 5266-1
Annual test Full rated duration discharge test Full rated duration discharge test BS 5266-1
Fire Door Gap Tolerances (BS 8214:2026 & FDIS Guidance)
Location Maximum Gap Notes Source
Head & Sides (jamb / lining) 3mm (max 4mm where certified to larger gap) Gap must be consistent — no peaks exceeding limit BS 8214:2026
Threshold (bottom of door) 8mm (or per certified threshold detail) Threshold seal / brush strip where fitted BS 8214:2026
Between double-leaf doors (meeting stiles) 3mm (max 4mm for certified assemblies) Co-ordinator required to ensure correct sequence BS 8214:2026
Building Height Thresholds — Key Regulatory Triggers
Threshold Triggered Requirement Source
11m (residential) Monthly fire door checks in common parts, quarterly lift checks, annual information requirements (FS(E) Regs 2022) FS(E) Regs 2022
11m (residential) Sprinklers required in new flats (AD B 2022 amendment) AD B Vol.1 (2022)
18m / 7 storeys (residential) Higher-Risk Building (HRB) — BSR registration, safety case, accountable person (BSA 2022) BSA 2022
18m (all) Non-combustible external wall materials required on new buildings (AD B B4) AD B
18m (residential) Additional FS(E) Regs 2022 requirements: FRS information boxes, wayfinding signage FS(E) Regs 2022
3 storeys (houses — England) Sprinklers required in new houses (AD B Vol.1) AD B Vol.1
All new dwellings (Wales) Sprinklers required in all new dwellings (Wales Building Regs) Wales AD B

📓 Notepad & Glossary
Your field notepad for client visits and site notes — with a professional glossary at the back covering key fire safety topics, responsible person duties, legislation, guidance, key numbers, and common deficiencies. Use this in conversation with clients without reaching for your phone.
