⚖️
Responsible Persons & Their Duties
RRFSO 2005 Art. 3 — who the RP is and their full legal duties across all categories
▼
Who Is the Responsible Person?

Under Art. 3 RRFSO 2005, the Responsible Person (RP) is:

  • In a workplace: the employer, if they have control of the premises
  • In any other case: the person who has control of the premises in connection with carrying on a trade, business or other undertaking (whether for profit or not)
  • If no person above: the owner of the premises
  • In practice: employer, building owner, landlord, freeholder, managing agent, facilities manager, or occupier with control
  • In multi-occupied buildings: there can be more than one RP — each with duties for their area
  • Under BSA 2022 s.156 (in force Oct 2023): all RPs must now record all FRA findings, fire safety arrangements, and identity of any assessor — regardless of building size

Source: RRFSO 2005 Art. 3; BSA 2022 s.156; RRFSO as amended

Core Legal Duties (RRFSO 2005)
  • Art. 9 — Carry out a suitable and sufficient fire risk assessment; review it regularly or when significant changes occur
  • Art. 9(4) — Record significant findings (now ALL findings under BSA 2022 s.156)
  • Art. 11 — Implement and maintain appropriate fire safety arrangements (including emergency procedures and evacuation plans)
  • Art. 12 — Eliminate or reduce fire risks as far as reasonably practicable; apply principles of prevention
  • Art. 13 — Provide appropriate fire detection and warning; maintain firefighting equipment
  • Art. 14 — Provide and maintain safe routes of escape; keep them clear and unobstructed
  • Art. 15 — Establish procedures for serious and imminent danger; nominate competent persons to implement evacuation
  • Art. 17 — Maintain fire safety measures in efficient working order
  • Art. 18 — Appoint competent persons to assist in fire safety compliance
  • Art. 19 — Provide relevant information to employees and others on premises
  • Art. 21 — Provide adequate fire safety training to all employees
  • Art. 22 — Co-operate and co-ordinate with other RPs sharing a building

Source: Regulatory Reform (Fire Safety) Order 2005 SI 2005/1541

Additional Duties — Multi-Occupied Residential (FS(E) Regs 2022)
  • Buildings >11m: monthly visual check of common parts fire doors (condition, gaps, self-closer, seals)
  • Buildings >11m: annual inspection of every flat entrance door — must offer access to residents
  • All multi-occupied residential: quarterly checks of lifts, evacuation alert systems, and fire doors in common parts
  • Display fire safety instructions in building entrances and lifts
  • Provide residents with fire safety information in accessible format
  • Buildings >18m: provide fire safety information to Fire & Rescue Service on request; place information box in building
  • Keep records of all checks and inspections

Source: Fire Safety (England) Regulations 2022 SI 2022/547; Gov.uk fire door guidance 2022

RPEEPs — From 6 January 2025
  • RPs of certain multi-occupied residential buildings must have a process to offer a personal emergency evacuation plan (PEEP) to any resident who may need assistance evacuating
  • Must keep records of offers made, agreements reached, and completed PEEPs
  • Plans must be reviewed periodically or when resident circumstances change
  • Applies to: all high-rise residential buildings (>18m or 7+ storeys) and specified mid-rise buildings (>11m)

Source: Fire Safety (Residential Evacuation Plans) (England) Regulations 2025; commenced 6 January 2025

Key Numbers
  • 5+ employees — previously triggered written FRA record; now all RPs must record under BSA 2022 s.156
  • 11m — threshold for monthly fire door checks and annual flat entrance door checks (FS(E) Regs 2022)
  • 18m / 7 storeys — higher-risk building definition; BSR regime applies; information box required (FS(E) Regs 2022)
  • 6 January 2025 — Residential Evacuation Plans Regulations 2025 in force
  • 1 October 2023 — BSA 2022 s.156 amendments to RRFSO in force (universal FRA recording)
  • Art. 32 — Failure to comply with general fire precaution duties: up to 2 years imprisonment and/or unlimited fine

Source: RRFSO 2005; BSA 2022; FS(E) Regs 2022; Res. Evac. Plans Regs 2025

Common Failures / Enforcement Triggers
  • No fire risk assessment in place or significantly out of date
  • FRA carried out by an incompetent person (no recognised qualification or experience)
  • Significant findings not recorded or not actioned
  • Fire safety arrangements not documented (now a legal requirement for all)
  • No fire safety training records for staff
  • Blocked or obstructed escape routes
  • Fire doors wedged open or self-closers removed
  • Monthly / annual checks not carried out or not recorded (FS(E) Regs buildings)
  • No process for RPEEPs in qualifying buildings (from Jan 2025)
  • Co-operation duty (Art. 22) not met in shared buildings
Responsible Person Duties — By Category
📋 FIRE RISK ASSESSMENT
  • Carry out a suitable and sufficient FRA (Art. 9 RRFSO 2005)
  • Record all significant findings and fire safety arrangements (BSA 2022 s.156 — now applies to ALL premises)
  • Review FRA when significant change occurs, after fire, or when no longer valid
  • Appoint only competent persons to conduct or assist with the FRA (Art. 18)
  • Share findings with employees and other RPs sharing the building (Arts. 19, 22)
RRFSO 2005 Art. 9; BSA 2022 s.156; PAS 79-1/79-2:2020
🚪 FIRE DOORS & PASSIVE FIRE PROTECTION
  • Maintain all fire doors in efficient working order — self-closing, no wedging (Art. 17)
  • Buildings >11m: monthly visual checks of common part fire doors (FS(E) Regs 2022)
  • Buildings >11m: annual check of every flat entrance door — offer access to residents
  • Ensure fire stopping integrity is maintained at all service penetrations
  • Appoint competent inspector (FDIS or equivalent) for thorough inspections
  • Keep records of all checks and inspections
RRFSO Art. 17; FS(E) Regs 2022 SI 2022/547; BS 8214:2026
🔔 FIRE DETECTION & WARNING
  • Provide and maintain appropriate fire detection and alarm system (Art. 13 RRFSO)
  • Ensure system is appropriate to the premises type and risk level
  • Weekly test of manual call points; periodic service to BS 5839-1 by competent engineer
  • Keep maintenance records; act promptly on defects
  • Ensure all occupants can hear and respond to alarm signal
RRFSO Art. 13; BS 5839-1:2017; FS(E) Regs 2022
🚪 ESCAPE ROUTES & EVACUATION
  • Provide and maintain adequate means of escape from all parts of the premises (Art. 14)
  • Keep escape routes clear and unobstructed at all times
  • Establish and maintain emergency procedures and evacuation plans (Art. 11)
  • Nominate competent persons to implement evacuation in serious/imminent danger (Art. 15)
  • Conduct fire drills at appropriate intervals; record outcomes (Art. 21, BS 9999)
  • From Jan 2025: process to offer RPEEPs to residents who need evacuation assistance
RRFSO Arts. 11, 14, 15; Res. Evac. Plans Regs 2025; AD B
💧 SUPPRESSION SYSTEMS
  • Maintain any installed sprinkler or suppression system in efficient working order (Art. 17)
  • Ensure annual service and inspection to BS EN 12845 (commercial) or BS 9251 (residential)
  • Keep service records; ensure no alterations made without competent engineer
  • Do not obstruct sprinkler heads or reduce coverage
RRFSO Art. 17; BS EN 12845; BS 9251; BS 9990 (risers)
🚒 DRY & WET RISERS / FIREFIGHTING
  • Maintain firefighting equipment in efficient working order (Art. 13 RRFSO)
  • Dry risers: annual BS 9990 test; wet risers: 6-monthly pressure test
  • Ensure firefighting shafts and lobbies are not obstructed
  • Keep landing valves clear and accessible; report defects promptly
  • Buildings >18m: provide fire safety information to FRS on request (FS(E) Regs 2022)
RRFSO Art. 13; FS(E) Regs 2022; BS 9990; AD B Vol.2
🪫 EMERGENCY LIGHTING
  • Provide and maintain emergency lighting on all escape routes (Art. 14; BS 5266-1)
  • Monthly functional test; annual full-duration test (3 hours); record results
  • Ensure luminaires provide minimum 1 lux on escape route floor level
  • Act promptly on any failed units; retain test records
RRFSO Art. 14; BS 5266-1:2016; BS EN 50172
🧯 FIRE EXTINGUISHERS
  • Provide appropriate extinguishers for the risk type and premises (Art. 13)
  • Annual service to BS 5306-3 by a competent person; 5-year extended service
  • Monthly visual check — pressure, condition, accessibility
  • Ensure staff know which extinguisher to use; include in fire training (Art. 21)
  • At least one 13A-rated extinguisher per 200m² of floor area; max 30m travel
RRFSO Art. 13; BS 5306-3:2017; BS 5306-8:2012
⚠️ SIGNAGE & NOTICES
  • Provide appropriate fire safety signs — exits, fire action, equipment location (Art. 19)
  • Signs must comply with Health & Safety (Safety Signs & Signals) Regs 1996
  • Illuminated or photoluminescent signs where necessary
  • FS(E) Regs 2022: display fire safety instructions in building entrances and lifts
  • Buildings >11m: instructions must include stay-put or evacuation strategy
RRFSO Art. 19; Safety Signs Regs 1996; FS(E) Regs 2022; ISO 7010
🏗️ HIGHER-RISK & HIGH-RISE BUILDINGS
  • Buildings >18m or 7+ storeys with 2+ residential units: full BSR regime applies
  • Register building with BSR; apply for building assessment certificate
  • As Accountable Person (AP): prepare and maintain a safety case
  • Report structural or fire safety occurrences (mandatory occurrence reporting)
  • Engage residents — provide safety information; establish residents panel if required
  • Co-operate with BSR inspections and enforcement actions
BSA 2022; RRFSO Art. 22; FS(E) Regs 2022; BSR guidance
🏛️ FIRE SAFETY MANAGEMENT
  • Implement and maintain fire safety arrangements covering prevention and protection (Art. 11)
  • Provide fire safety training to all employees on induction and when risk changes (Art. 21)
  • Co-operate and co-ordinate with other RPs sharing the building (Art. 22)
  • Maintain a fire safety logbook — FRA, checks, drills, training, maintenance records
  • Ensure incoming RPs receive all relevant fire safety information on change of occupier
  • Review and update all arrangements when significant changes occur
RRFSO Arts. 11, 21, 22; BSA 2022 s.156; PAS 79-1/79-2
🚪
Fire Doors
FD30S / FD60S — compartmentation, escape routes, flat entrances
▼
Key Legislation
  • RRFSO 2005 Art. 14 — escape routes must be maintained; fire doors are part of this
  • RRFSO Art. 17 — maintenance duty: fire doors must be kept in efficient working order
  • Fire Safety Act 2021 — flat entrance doors and structure now explicitly in RRFSO scope
  • FS(E) Regs 2022 — monthly checks of fire doors in common parts (buildings >11m); annual checks of flat entrance doors
  • Building Regs 2010 Part B — fire door specifications for new build and change of use
Key Guidance & Standards
  • BS 8214:2026 — current standard for fire door assemblies (supersedes 2016 edition)
  • BS EN 1634-1 — fire resistance test standard for door sets
  • Gov.uk FS(E) Regs Fire Door Guidance — inspection duties guidance (Aug 2025)
  • FDIS / BWF / BM TRADA — industry guidance on inspection and certification
  • Third-party certification: Certifire, UKAS, BWF CERTIFIRE
Responsible Person & Their Duties

WHO IS RESPONSIBLE?

  • Non-domestic premises: the employer or person with control of the premises (RRFSO 2005 Art. 3)
  • Residential blocks (common parts): the freeholder, managing agent, or RTM company
  • Individual flats: the leaseholder is responsible for their own flat entrance door
  • Where multiple RPs share a building: each RP for their area; must co-operate (Art. 22)

WHAT THEY MUST DO

  • Ensure all fire doors are self-closing, correctly rated, undamaged, and properly maintained (Art. 17)
  • Buildings >11m: monthly visual check of all common parts fire doors — condition, gaps, self-closer, seals, signage (FS(E) Regs 2022)
  • Buildings >11m: annual check of every flat entrance door — must offer access to residents
  • Keep written records of all checks — date, outcome, actions taken
  • Ensure fire doors are never wedged open unless hold-open device is linked to alarm system
  • Appoint a competent inspector (FDIS-certified or equivalent) for thorough inspections
  • Act on defects promptly — a non-compliant fire door must not remain in service

RRFSO 2005 Arts. 3, 17, 22; Fire Safety Act 2021; FS(E) Regs 2022 SI 2022/547

Key Numbers
  • FD30S — 30 min integrity + smoke seal (most common in residential)
  • FD60S — 60 min integrity + smoke seal (stair enclosures, high-rise)
  • Gap tolerance: top & sides max 3mm; bottom max 8mm (or threshold seal)
  • Intumescent strip: activates at approximately 150°C to seal gaps
  • Self-closer: must close door fully from 90° to latched position unaided
  • Hinges: minimum 3; steel; melting point >800°C
  • Source: BS 8214:2026 Clauses 7, 10, 13; LACORS Housing Fire Safety 2008; FS(E) Regs 2022 guidance
Common Deficiencies
  • Self-closer removed or ineffective — door doesn't fully close
  • Excessive gaps — failed intumescent strip or distorted door
  • Door propped open with wedge or furniture
  • Missing or damaged intumescent strip / smoke seal
  • Letterbox in fire door without intumescent protection
  • Door not certified — no label or Certifire certificate
  • Glazing panel not fire-rated glass
  • Frame not fire-rated or not properly fixed
📋
Fire Risk Assessment
RRFSO Art. 9 duty — suitable and sufficient FRA
▼
Key Legislation
  • RRFSO 2005 Art. 9 — duty on RP to carry out suitable and sufficient FRA
  • RRFSO Art. 9(3) — FRA must be reviewed regularly or when significant change
  • RRFSO Art. 9(4) — must record significant findings (5+ employees or licensed premises)
  • Fire Safety Act 2021 — FRA scope extended to structure, external walls, flat entrance doors
Key Guidance
  • PAS 79-2:2020 — FRA methodology for housing
  • PAS 79-1:2020 — FRA methodology for non-domestic
  • Gov.uk FSRA guides — premises-specific FRA guidance (offices, care homes etc.)
  • NFCC Protection Competency Framework
  • BAFE SP205 — third-party certification for FRA organisations
Responsible Person & Their Duties

WHO IS RESPONSIBLE?

  • Workplaces: the employer (if they have control of the premises)
  • All other premises: the person who has control in connection with carrying on a trade or business
  • Where no employer or controller: the owner of the premises
  • Residential blocks (common parts only): freeholder, managing agent, or RTM company
  • There can be more than one RP — each responsible for their area; must co-operate (Art. 22)

WHAT THEY MUST DO

  • Carry out a suitable and sufficient fire risk assessment of the premises (Art. 9)
  • Record all significant findings and fire safety arrangements — now applies to ALL premises regardless of size (BSA 2022 s.156, in force Oct 2023)
  • Record the identity of any person appointed to conduct or review the FRA
  • Review the FRA when: significant change, fire or near-miss occurs, reason to suspect it is no longer valid
  • Appoint only a competent person to conduct or assist with the FRA (Art. 18)
  • Communicate findings to employees and share relevant information with other RPs (Arts. 19, 22)
  • Implement all significant findings as far as reasonably practicable

RRFSO 2005 Arts. 3, 9, 18, 19, 22; BSA 2022 s.156; PAS 79-1:2020 / PAS 79-2:2020

Key Numbers
  • 5 steps: Identify hazards → identify people at risk → evaluate/remove/reduce → record → review
  • Source: RRFSO 2005 Art. 9; PAS 79-1:2020 & PAS 79-2:2020; BSA 2022 s.156
  • Review trigger: significant change, post-fire, or periodically (no statutory interval — risk-based)
  • Written record: required if 5+ employees or licensed/registered premises
  • Competency: BAFE SP205, MIFSM, MIFireE, or demonstrable experience
Common Deficiencies
  • FRA not reviewed following Fire Safety Act 2021 — external walls not in scope
  • Assessor not competent — no third-party certification
  • Significant findings not implemented or actioned
  • No PEEP process for vulnerable occupants
  • FRA not shared with employees or residents
  • PAS 79 format not followed for residential blocks
🧱
Fire Stopping & Compartmentation
Service penetrations, cavity barriers, compartment walls and floors
▼
Key Legislation
  • RRFSO Art. 14 — escape routes free of fire and smoke
  • RRFSO Art. 17 — maintenance of fire precautions including compartmentation
  • Building Regs 2010 Part B — compartmentation requirements B3
  • Fire Safety Act 2021 — structure and external walls in FRA scope
Key Guidance
  • BS EN 1366-3 — penetration seal testing standard
  • FIS Guide — Firestopping of Service Penetrations (in library)
  • ASFP Blue Book — fire resisting ductwork (in library)
  • AD B Vol.1 & 2 — compartmentation requirements
  • NHBC / LABC — guidance for residential
Responsible Person & Their Duties

WHO IS RESPONSIBLE?

  • Occupied buildings: the RP under RRFSO 2005 — employer, person with control, or owner
  • During construction / refurbishment: the Principal Contractor under CDM 2015 is responsible for installed fire stopping during the works; the Principal Designer must co-ordinate passive fire protection in the design
  • At handover: the RP (building owner / managing agent) takes responsibility for all installed fire stopping and must receive Regulation 38 documentation
  • Higher-risk buildings: the Accountable Person under BSA 2022 must maintain the safety case including compartmentation evidence

WHAT THEY MUST DO

  • Ensure all compartment walls, floors, and fire stopping are intact and maintained (RRFSO Art. 17)
  • Commission intrusive compartmentation surveys where integrity cannot be verified visually — particularly in older buildings
  • Ensure all service penetrations (pipes, cables, ducts) are fire-stopped with third-party certified products (FIRAS, IFC, LPCB)
  • Use only FIRAS or IFC certified contractors for installation and remediation
  • Obtain and retain product certificates, field of application reports, and installation records for all penetration seals
  • Include compartmentation status in the FRA; review after any building works

RRFSO 2005 Art. 17; CDM 2015; BSA 2022; Building Regs 2010 Reg. 38; ASFP guidance

Key Numbers
  • REI 60 — compartment walls/floors between flats (BS 9991)
  • REI 30 — minimum for escape route protection
  • Cavity barriers: every 20m in cavities, or at compartment boundaries
  • Max gap: any unsealed penetration is a failure — zero tolerance
  • Tested systems: product must be installed per manufacturer's tested detail
Common Deficiencies
  • Cable and pipe penetrations through compartment walls — no fire stopping
  • Redundant openings — old ductwork, pipes not removed and sealed
  • Fire stopping installed incorrectly — wrong product for substrate
  • Ceiling voids — no cavity barriers, open above fire doors
  • Risers and service shafts — not compartmented at each floor
  • Conversion properties — pre-existing voids in floors unknown
🔔
Fire Alarm & Detection Systems
BS 5839-1 — categories L1–L5, P1–P2, M
▼
Key Legislation
  • RRFSO Art. 13 — fire detection and warning systems must be provided where necessary
  • RRFSO Art. 17 — maintenance of fire detection systems
  • FS(E) Regs 2022 — annual inspection of fire alarm in residential buildings; records kept
  • Smoke & CO Alarm Regs 2022 — smoke alarm every storey; CO alarm in rooms with combustion appliances
Key Guidance
  • BS 5839-1:2017 — design, installation, commissioning and maintenance (non-domestic)
  • BS 5839-6:2019 — domestic premises (Grades A–F, Categories LD1–LD3, PD1–PD3)
  • FIA Guidance — category selection tool (in library)
  • BAFE SP203 — third-party certification for alarm companies
  • Categories: L1 (full), L2 (escape + high risk), L3 (escape routes only), M (manual only)
Responsible Person & Their Duties

WHO IS RESPONSIBLE?

  • Non-domestic premises: the employer or person with control of the premises (RRFSO Art. 3)
  • Common parts of residential blocks: the freeholder, managing agent, or RTM company
  • Shared buildings: the RP who has control of the common alarm system maintains it; all other RPs must co-operate (Art. 22)

WHAT THEY MUST DO

  • Provide and maintain an appropriate fire detection and alarm system for the risk (Art. 13 RRFSO)
  • Weekly: test at least one manual call point, rotating zones; record in logbook
  • 6-monthly: inspection and service by a BAFE SP203-certified contractor
  • Annual: full system inspection, test, and service to BS 5839-1
  • Maintain a fire alarm logbook — all test results, faults, and engineer visits
  • Ensure all occupants can hear and respond to the alarm signal
  • Never disable or silence the alarm without a controlled procedure and competent supervision
  • Act promptly on any fault — a defective system must be remedied immediately

RRFSO 2005 Arts. 3, 13, 22; BS 5839-1:2017; BAFE SP203-1; FS(E) Regs 2022

Key Numbers
  • 75dB(A) — minimum at bedhead for sleeping premises
  • 65dB(A) — minimum in occupied areas generally
  • Weekly — call point test frequency
  • 6 monthly / annual — full service by competent person
  • 7.5m radius — standard smoke detector coverage (flat ceiling)
  • L1 — full building; L2 — escape + specified areas; L3 — escape routes only
Common Deficiencies
  • No logbook or test records
  • Detectors painted over or covered
  • System not serviced in last 12 months
  • Alarm not audible throughout building
  • Wrong detector type for environment (e.g. optical in kitchen)
  • No remote monitoring — faults not reported
  • Panel showing faults — not investigated
💧
Sprinkler Systems
BS EN 12845 (commercial) / BS 9251 (residential)
▼
Key Legislation
  • Building Regs 2010 Part B / AD B — sprinklers required in new residential buildings >11m (2022 amendment)
  • BSA 2022 — sprinklers considered within safety case for HRBs
  • Wales — mandatory in all new residential buildings since 2016
  • RRFSO Art. 17 — maintenance of suppression systems
Key Guidance
  • BS EN 12845 — fixed firefighting systems, automatic sprinklers (commercial)
  • BS 9251:2021 — residential and domestic sprinkler systems
  • BAFSA — British Automatic Fire Sprinkler Association guidance
  • Loss Prevention Standard LPS 1048 — sprinkler installer approval
  • FRS often provide free surveys for high-risk premises
Responsible Person & Their Duties

WHO IS RESPONSIBLE?

  • Commercial / non-domestic: the employer or person with control of the premises (RRFSO Art. 3)
  • Residential blocks (common parts): the freeholder, managing agent, or RTM company
  • Within individual dwellings: the leaseholder or occupier is responsible for the system within their dwelling
  • New higher-risk buildings: the Accountable Person once occupied (BSA 2022)

WHAT THEY MUST DO

  • Maintain the system in efficient working order at all times (RRFSO Art. 17)
  • Weekly: visual check of all control valves — ensure open and not tampered; record in logbook
  • Annual: full inspection and test by a competent contractor (BAFSA member recommended) to BS EN 12845 or BS 9251
  • Keep complete maintenance records — all inspections, faults, and remedial works
  • Never obstruct, paint over, or modify sprinkler heads without engineer approval
  • Ensure adequate water supply and pressure is maintained at all times
  • Report any activation, damage, or tampering immediately

RRFSO 2005 Arts. 3, 13, 17; BS EN 12845; BS 9251; BSA 2022; BAFSA guidance

Key Numbers
  • 11m — threshold for mandatory sprinklers in new residential (England, 2022)
  • 68°C — standard sprinkler head activation temperature
  • Weekly — visual inspection of valves
  • Annual — full test and service
  • BS 9251 — residential; BS EN 12845 — commercial/industrial
  • Each head covers approx. 9–12m² depending on hazard class
Common Deficiencies
  • Heads painted over — will not activate correctly
  • Items stored within 500mm below heads — obstructs spray pattern
  • Control valves closed or partially closed
  • No maintenance records or logbook
  • System installed but not commissioned or tested
  • Heads damaged or corroded
🚒
Dry Risers & Fire Fighting Facilities
BS 9990 — for FRS use in buildings over 18m
▼
Key Legislation
  • Building Regs 2010 Part B / AD B — dry risers required in buildings with floors >18m above fire service access level
  • RRFSO Art. 13 — firefighting equipment must be provided and maintained
  • RRFSO Art. 17 — maintenance duty
  • FS(E) Regs 2022 — quarterly checks of lifts for FRS use (buildings >18m)
Key Guidance
  • BS 9990:2015 — non-automatic fire fighting systems (dry and wet risers)
  • AD B Vol.2 B5 — access and facilities for the fire service
  • FRS local guidance — inlet location, access requirements vary by FRS
  • Wet riser — required in buildings >50m (permanently charged)
  • Dry riser — required 18m–50m (charged by FRS pump)
Responsible Person & Their Duties

WHO IS RESPONSIBLE?

  • Buildings with dry or wet risers: the RP under RRFSO 2005 — typically the building owner, freeholder, or managing agent
  • Residential blocks: the freeholder or managing agent — risers in common areas cannot be delegated to leaseholders
  • Multi-tenanted commercial buildings: the landlord or managing agent retains responsibility for shared firefighting infrastructure
  • Under FS(E) Regs 2022: in buildings >18m, the RP must provide fire safety information to the FRS on request — including riser locations and maintenance records

WHAT THEY MUST DO

  • Maintain all firefighting equipment in efficient working order (RRFSO Art. 13)
  • Dry risers: annual pressure test and inspection by a competent contractor to BS 9990
  • Wet risers: 6-monthly pressure test; annual full inspection to BS 9990
  • Ensure inlet boxes and landing valves are accessible, unobstructed, and not locked
  • Ensure landing valves are not seized, painted over, or damaged
  • Keep maintenance records — test dates, engineer reports, and remedial actions
  • Report damage or tampering immediately; arrange emergency repair

RRFSO 2005 Arts. 3, 13, 17; FS(E) Regs 2022; BS 9990:2015; AD B Vol.2

Key Numbers
  • 18m — threshold for dry riser requirement
  • 50m — threshold for wet riser requirement
  • Annual — pressure test to 10 bar for 15 minutes
  • 18m — max distance from FRS vehicle to inlet
  • Quarterly — visual inspection recommended
  • Inlet: instantaneous 64mm female coupling (standard UK)
Common Deficiencies
  • Inlet cabinet locked or obstructed — FRS cannot connect
  • No annual pressure test record
  • Inlet damaged or coupling seized
  • FRS vehicle access obstructed — parking, landscaping, gates
  • Outlet valves seized — not operated in years
  • System modified without recertification
🚪
Escape Routes & Means of Escape
RRFSO Art. 14 — routes to places of ultimate safety
▼
Key Legislation
  • RRFSO Art. 14 — adequate number and capacity of escape routes; clear, unobstructed, signed, lit
  • RRFSO Art. 14(2)(f) — routes must lead to place of safety outside
  • RRFSO Art. 17 — maintenance of escape routes
  • Building Regs Part B — travel distances, exit widths, stair capacities
  • Highways Act 1980 s.97 — external lighting responsibility
Key Guidance
  • AD B Vol.1 & 2 — travel distances by purpose group
  • BS 9999:2017 — escape route design for non-domestic
  • BS 9991:2015 — residential escape route design
  • BS 5266-1 — emergency lighting
  • BS ISO 23601 / BS EN ISO 7010 — escape route signs
Responsible Person & Their Duties

WHO IS RESPONSIBLE?

  • Non-domestic premises: the employer or person with control of the premises (RRFSO Art. 3)
  • Common parts of residential blocks: the freeholder, managing agent, or RTM company — escape routes in common areas are their responsibility
  • Within individual flats: the leaseholder or occupier is responsible for internal escape routes in their own dwelling
  • Shared buildings: each RP responsible for escape routes within their area; must co-ordinate with other RPs (Art. 22)

WHAT THEY MUST DO

  • Provide and maintain adequate means of escape from all parts of the premises (Art. 14)
  • Keep all escape routes clear and unobstructed at all times — no storage, no locked doors on escape routes
  • Ensure escape routes are adequately lit — emergency lighting where required (Art. 14)
  • Establish, implement and maintain written emergency procedures and evacuation plans (Art. 11)
  • Conduct fire drills at appropriate intervals; record outcomes (Art. 21)
  • Nominate competent persons to implement evacuation in serious or imminent danger (Art. 15)
  • From 6 January 2025: process to offer RPEEPs to residents who need evacuation assistance (Res. Evac. Plans Regs 2025)
  • Maintain all escape route signage in good condition (Art. 19)

RRFSO 2005 Arts. 3, 11, 14, 15, 19, 21, 22; Res. Evac. Plans Regs 2025; AD B

Key Numbers — Travel Distances
  • Office — one direction: 18m; two directions: 45m
  • Shop — one direction: 18m; two directions: 45m
  • Assembly — one direction: 15m; two directions: 32m
  • Residential flats: 7.5m dead-end corridor to stair
  • Hotel bedroom corridor: 9m dead-end; 18m two directions
  • Industrial — one direction: 25m; two directions: 45m
Common Deficiencies
  • Escape routes obstructed — storage, furniture, equipment
  • Emergency lighting not tested — no monthly flick test records
  • External escape route poorly lit — check street authority via FindMyStreet
  • Final exit locked or requires key to open during occupied hours
  • Assembly point not signed or too close to building
  • Travel distance exceeds maximum for purpose group
  • No alternative escape from upper floors
💡
Emergency Lighting
BS 5266-1 — escape route and open area lighting
▼
Key Legislation
  • RRFSO Art. 14 — escape routes must be lit; emergency lighting where necessary
  • RRFSO Art. 17 — maintenance duty
  • Workplace Regs 1992 Reg. 8 — suitable and sufficient lighting including emergency
  • Building Regs Part B — emergency lighting in certain buildings
Key Guidance
  • BS 5266-1:2016 — emergency lighting design, installation and maintenance
  • BS EN 50172 — emergency escape lighting systems
  • ICEL — Industry Committee for Emergency Lighting guidance
  • Self-contained vs. central battery systems
  • Maintained vs. non-maintained luminaires
Responsible Person & Their Duties

WHO IS RESPONSIBLE?

  • Non-domestic premises: the employer or person with control of the premises (RRFSO Art. 3)
  • Common parts of residential blocks: the freeholder, managing agent, or RTM company — emergency lighting in stairwells, corridors, and lobbies is the RP's responsibility
  • Individual flats: emergency lighting is not typically required within individual dwellings; the occupier has no specific duty here under RRFSO

WHAT THEY MUST DO

  • Provide and maintain adequate emergency lighting on all escape routes (RRFSO Art. 14; BS 5266-1)
  • Monthly: short-duration functional test (flick test) of all luminaires; record pass/fail in logbook
  • Annual: full rated-duration test — minimum 3 hours for sustained luminaires; record results
  • Replace any failed luminaires promptly — a failed unit on an escape route is a compliance failure
  • Maintain a test logbook — dates, results, remedial actions, and service records
  • Ensure minimum illuminance of 1 lux on the escape route floor level (BS EN 1838)
  • Ensure coverage extends to all escape routes, stairwells, plant rooms, and final exits

RRFSO 2005 Arts. 3, 14, 17; BS 5266-1:2016; BS EN 1838:2013; BS EN 50172:2004

Key Numbers
  • 1 lux — minimum on escape route floor (centre line)
  • 0.5 lux — minimum in open areas (anti-panic lighting)
  • 3 hours — minimum duration for escape lighting
  • Monthly — short function test
  • Annual — full 3-hour discharge test
  • 2m — maximum height for luminaire above floor on escape route
Common Deficiencies
  • No monthly test records — common in small premises
  • Failed luminaires not replaced — dark spots on escape route
  • Insufficient coverage — stairwells, final exits, plant rooms
  • Battery life expired — unit illuminates briefly then fails
  • No emergency lighting in basement or windowless areas
  • Changed layout — luminaires not repositioned
🧯
Fire Extinguishers
BS 5306-3 — selection, installation and maintenance
▼
Key Legislation & Standards
  • RRFSO Art. 13 — appropriate firefighting equipment must be provided
  • BS 5306-3:2017 — selection and installation
  • BS 5306-8 — maintenance
  • BS EN 3 — portable fire extinguishers (European standard)
  • BAFE SP101 — competency scheme for extinguisher companies
Types & Uses
TypeClassUse
WaterAPaper, wood, textiles
Foam (AFFF)A, BLiquids, general
CO₂B, electricalElectrical equipment
Dry PowderA, B, CMulti-risk, flammable gas
Wet ChemicalA, FCooking oils, Class F
Responsible Person & Their Duties

WHO IS RESPONSIBLE?

  • Non-domestic premises: the employer or person with control of the premises (RRFSO Art. 3)
  • Common parts of residential blocks: the freeholder or managing agent — extinguishers in common areas are the RP's responsibility
  • Individual tenants / occupiers: responsible for extinguishers within their own demised area where they have control of that space
  • Note: fire extinguishers are not typically required within individual residential flats — this is a non-domestic and common area duty

WHAT THEY MUST DO

  • Provide appropriate extinguishers for the type and risk of the premises (RRFSO Art. 13)
  • Monthly: visual check — pressure gauge, condition, signage, accessibility; record in logbook
  • Annual: service by a competent person to BS 5306-3 (BAFE SP101 certified preferred)
  • 5-yearly: extended service (or per manufacturer) — discharge and recharge
  • Ensure correct type: CO₂ for electrical; water/foam for Class A; dry powder for multi-risk
  • Train staff on which extinguisher to use, when to use it, and when to evacuate instead (Art. 21)
  • Replace or re-service immediately after any use

RRFSO 2005 Arts. 3, 13, 17, 21; BS 5306-3:2017; BS 5306-8:2012; BAFE SP101

Key Numbers
  • 1 x 13A extinguisher per 200m² floor area minimum
  • Max travel distance: 30m to nearest extinguisher
  • Annual — service and inspection
  • 5 years — extended service (discharge test) for water/foam
  • 10 years — CO₂ hydraulic test
Common Deficiencies
  • Wrong type for risk — CO₂ only where Class A risk is present
  • No annual service — no service label or label out of date
  • Extinguisher obstructed or not signed
  • No Class F extinguisher in commercial kitchen
  • Pressure gauge in red — discharged or faulty
  • Staff not trained — wouldn't know how to use it safely
🚨
Fire Safety Signage & Notices
BS EN ISO 7010 — safety signs; RRFSO Art. 14 signing duty
▼
Key Legislation
  • RRFSO Art. 14(2)(e) — escape routes must be indicated by signs
  • Health & Safety (Safety Signs) Regs 1996 — prescribes sign types and colours
  • FS(E) Regs 2022 — wayfinding signage (floor IDs, door numbers) in high-rise residential
  • Building Regs Part B — signage as part of escape route design
Key Standards
  • BS EN ISO 7010 — graphical symbols for safety signs (fire safety: E001–E023)
  • BS ISO 23601 — evacuation and emergency signs
  • Green — escape route and first aid signs
  • Red — fire equipment location signs
  • White on blue — mandatory (e.g. Fire Door Keep Shut)
Responsible Person & Their Duties

WHO IS RESPONSIBLE?

  • Non-domestic premises: the employer or person with control of the premises (RRFSO Art. 3)
  • Common parts of residential blocks: the freeholder or managing agent — fire safety signage in common areas is their responsibility
  • Multi-tenanted buildings: each RP responsible for signage within their demised area; building RP responsible for common areas
  • Under FS(E) Regs 2022: in residential blocks >11m, the RP must display fire safety instructions at building entrances and in every lift

WHAT THEY MUST DO

  • Provide appropriate fire safety signs on all escape routes, at fire exits, and at firefighting equipment locations (RRFSO Art. 19)
  • Signs must comply with Health & Safety (Safety Signs & Signals) Regulations 1996 — green for escape, red for firefighting equipment
  • Provide illuminated or photoluminescent signs where visibility in an emergency cannot otherwise be guaranteed
  • Ensure fire action notices are displayed prominently throughout the premises
  • Buildings >11m: display fire safety instructions including the evacuation strategy (stay-put or simultaneous) in all building entrances and lifts (FS(E) Regs 2022)
  • Inspect signage regularly — replace damaged, faded, or missing signs promptly

RRFSO 2005 Arts. 3, 19; Safety Signs & Signals Regs 1996 SI 1996/341; FS(E) Regs 2022; ISO 7010

Common Deficiencies
  • Missing escape route signs — particularly at changes of direction
  • Fire action notice out of date — wrong assembly point or telephone number
  • Fire door signs missing — occupants unaware doors must be kept shut
  • Signs not illuminated or photoluminescent where required
  • Assembly point not signed externally
  • High-rise — no floor identification or wayfinding for FRS
Premises Guide
Comprehensive fire safety reference by premises type — occupancy calculations, travel distances, evacuation strategies, detection categories, key legislation and guidance, and common FRA findings. All figures referenced against current AD B, BS 9999, BS 9991, and NFCC guidance. For professional reference only — not legal advice. Always verify against current legislation and apply your own expert judgement.
🏬
Purpose-Built Blocks of Flats
Multi-occupied residential buildings — common parts under RRFSO, flats under Housing Act 2004
RRFSO 2005 Fire Safety Act 2021 FS(E) Regs 2022 BS 9991:2015 PAS 79-2:2020
▼
Key Legislation
  • RRFSO 2005 — common parts FRA duty on Responsible Person (Art. 9)
  • Fire Safety Act 2021 — structure, external walls, flat entrance doors now in scope
  • FS(E) Regs 2022 — monthly common parts fire door checks (>11m), annual flat entrance door checks, quarterly lift checks (>18m)
  • Building Safety Act 2022 — Higher-Risk Building regime (>18m / 7 storeys)
  • Housing Act 2004 — HHSRS Category 1 fire hazards in flats enforceable by LA
  • Res. Evac. Plans Regs 2025 — PEEP process for residents needing assistance
Key Standards & Guidance
  • BS 9991:2015+A1:2021 — fire safety design & management of residential buildings
  • PAS 79-2:2020 — FRA methodology for housing
  • AD B Vol.1 (2019+2022) — means of escape, compartmentation, sprinklers (>11m)
  • BS 8214:2026 — fire door assemblies
  • LGA Guide — Fire Safety in Purpose-Built Blocks of Flats
  • NFCC — Fire Safety in Specialised Housing
Occupancy Calculation

For common parts (corridors, stairs, lobbies) occupancy is not directly calculated — design is based on flat count and floor area. For FRA risk assessment purposes:

Assumed occupancy = No. of flats × avg. household size (2.3 persons, ONS)

For communal facilities (if any): use 1 person per 1m² for standing areas or table/seating count for dining areas.

For means of escape calculations refer to BS 9991 Table 9 — stair widths based on simultaneous or phased evacuation.

Travel Distances (AD B Vol.1 / BS 9991)
LocationMax Distance
Dead-end corridor (within flat)7.5m to front door
Common corridor (to stair)7.5m dead-end / 30m with 2 directions
Within staircase enclosureNo limit — protected stair

Source: AD B Vol.1 / BS 9991:2015 Section 9

Evacuation Strategy
  • Stay-put (Defend-in-Place) — default for purpose-built blocks with 60-min compartmentation. Residents in unaffected flats remain. Only occupants of fire-affected flat evacuate initially.
  • Simultaneous Evacuation — adopted where compartmentation is compromised or stay-put no longer appropriate. Requires reassessment of stair widths and escape routes.
  • Temporary Simultaneous Evacuation — interim measure while remediation works are undertaken. Requires waking watch or alarm system.
  • Trigger for review: fires in multiple flats, structural compartmentation failures, cladding defects, resident vulnerabilities.

Ref: BS 9991 Section 14 / NFCC guidance

Fire Detection (BS 5839-6)
  • Common parts (non-domestic): BS 5839-1:2017 Category L3 minimum (detection on escape routes) — Category L2 recommended (detection throughout common areas). Grade terminology (A/D) does not apply to common parts — those are BS 5839-6 domestic terms.
  • Within flats (domestic): BS 5839-6:2019 Grade D Category LD2 minimum — mains-powered interlinked smoke alarms on each storey plus heat alarm in kitchen. Grade A (full panel) for higher-risk conversions.
  • High-rise (>18m): BS 5839-1 Category L2 or L1 in common parts — addressable system strongly recommended.
  • FS(E) Regs 2022: Fire alarm system inspected annually by competent person; records kept on-site.
Common FRA Findings
  • Fire doors — excessive gaps, failed self-closers, missing intumescent strips, propped open
  • Compartmentation breaches — service penetrations, redundant openings, letterboxes in flat entrance doors
  • Combustible materials stored in common parts (bikes, furniture, buggies)
  • Emergency lighting deficiencies — failed fittings, inadequate coverage
  • No PEEP process for vulnerable residents
  • Stay-put strategy not communicated to residents
  • External wall system not assessed post Fire Safety Act 2021
  • External escape routes — inadequate lighting to assembly point. Check street authority via FindMyStreet.co.uk where lighting is on adopted highway
Sheltered Housing / Extra Care / Supported Living Sub-type ▼
Key Legislation
  • RRFSO 2005 — common parts FRA
  • Care Act 2014 — safeguarding duties for vulnerable residents
  • Health & Social Care Act 2008 — CQC registration for care-providing schemes
  • Res. Evac. Plans Regs 2025 — PEEP obligations significantly heightened
Evacuation Considerations
  • Resident mobility assessments essential — PEEPs for all who cannot self-evacuate
  • Progressive Horizontal Evacuation (PHE) may be appropriate where full simultaneous evacuation is impractical
  • Warden/scheme manager role critical — training records required
  • Out-of-hours cover arrangements must be documented in Fire Emergency Plan
  • Refuge areas — must be provided where simultaneous evacuation not adopted
Key Guidance
  • NFCC — Fire Safety in Specialised Housing (free)
  • Gov.uk — FSRA Residential Care Premises guide
  • BS 9991:2015 — sheltered housing provisions (Section 16)
🏘
Houses in Multiple Occupation (HMOs)
Bedsits, shared houses, converted houses — RRFSO + Housing Act 2004 dual enforcement
RRFSO 2005 Housing Act 2004 LACORS 2009
▼
Key Legislation
  • RRFSO 2005 — applies to common parts of HMOs with non-domestic elements. Art. 9 FRA duty on RP.
  • Housing Act 2004 — HHSRS fire hazard assessment; mandatory HMO licensing for larger HMOs (5+ occupants, 3+ storeys or 3+ storeys in some LAs)
  • Management of Houses in Multiple Occupation (England) Regs 2006 — management duties including fire safety equipment maintenance
  • Smoke & CO Alarm Regs 2022 — smoke alarm every floor, CO alarm in rooms with fixed combustion appliances
Occupancy & Floor Area Assessment

Minimum room sizes under Housing Act / HMO licensing:

UseMin Floor Area
Single person sleeping room6.51m²
Two-person sleeping room10.22m²
Child (under 10) sleeping room4.64m²
Max occupancy = Total habitable floor area ÷ min room size standard

For fire risk — use actual room occupancy as per tenancy agreements for FRA, not theoretical maximum.

Detection (BS 5839-6 / LACORS)
HMO TypeMin GradeCategory
Low-risk shared house (2 storeys)Grade DLD3
Standard shared houseGrade DLD2
Higher-risk / bedsitsGrade ALD2
Large HMO / complex layoutGrade ALD1

Ref: LACORS Housing Fire Safety 2009

Escape Routes (LACORS / AD B)
  • Protected escape route from each floor to final exit — 30-min fire-resisting construction
  • Maximum travel distance in common areas: 7.5m dead-end to protected stairway
  • Fire doors to all habitable rooms opening onto escape route — FD20S minimum for low-risk, FD30S for higher-risk
  • No cooking facilities opening directly onto single escape stair without lobby protection
Common FRA Findings
  • Inadequate fire detection grade for risk level
  • Kitchen opening directly onto sole escape stair — no lobby or fire door
  • Escape routes obstructed or reduced in width
  • No fire doors to habitable rooms (or inadequate standard)
  • Electrical installation not periodically inspected (EICR overdue)
  • No documented fire emergency plan or tenant fire safety information
🔄
Conversions & Change of Use
Houses to flats, commercial to residential, material change of use — Building Regs 2010 Regulation 4
Building Regs 2010 RRFSO 2005 AD B Vol.1
▼
Key Legislation
  • Building Regulations 2010 — Regulation 4 (material change of use) triggers compliance with Parts B, A, E, F, H, J, L, M, P as applicable
  • AD B Vol.1 — applies to converted dwellings. Where full compliance with new-build standard is impractical, reasonable provision must be made
  • The Building (Approved Inspectors etc.) Regs 2010 — Building Notice or Full Plans application required for conversion works
  • RRFSO 2005 — once occupied, common parts FRA duty applies immediately
  • Regulatory Reform Order 2005 Reg. 38 — fire safety information must be handed to RP on completion
Conversion Types & Key Requirements
Conversion TypeKey RequirementStandard
House → flats (up to 3 storeys)Protected escape stair, 30-min compartmentation between flats, fire doorsAD B Vol.1
House → flats (4+ storeys)Protected escape stair, smoke detection LD2 minimum, 60-min compartmentationAD B / BS 9991
Commercial → residentialFull Part B compliance for residential — compartmentation, escape, alarmsAD B Vol.1
Single dwelling → HMOLACORS guidance, protected escape route, fire doors, detection upgradeLACORS 2009
Existing Buildings — Assessment Approach

For existing converted buildings where original building control records may be unavailable:

  • Intrusive surveys required to establish compartmentation integrity — open up and check
  • Fire stopping at service penetrations must be verified, not assumed
  • BS 9991 / LACORS — use as benchmark for existing stock where full AD B compliance cannot be achieved
  • Reasonable provision — the standard for existing buildings under Regulation 4. Document any deviations and compensatory measures
Common FRA Findings in Converted Properties
  • Inadequate compartmentation between original rooms now used as flats
  • Timber floor/ceiling construction with no fire stopping — pre-1919 builds particularly at risk
  • Shared services (gas, electricity, plumbing) creating compartmentation breaches
  • Original staircase not enclosed as protected route
  • Cellar / basement storage not fire-separated from escape route
  • No building control completion certificate — unknown specification
💼
Offices
Purpose Group 4 (Office) — AD B Vol.2 / BS 9999
RRFSO 2005 BS 9999:2017 AD B Vol.2 DCLG Offices Guide
▼
Occupancy Calculation

BS 9999:2017 Table B.1 — Occupancy Factor (OF)

Office TypeFloor Area per Person
General office (open plan)6m² per person
Cellular office6–12m² per person
Call centre / dense occupancy3–4m² per person (guidance — not specified in BS 9999 Table B.1; use actual desk count where possible)
Meeting rooms1 per seat
Occupancy = Net floor area (m²) ÷ occupancy factor

Net floor area = gross floor area minus structural walls, plant rooms, toilets, stairwells.

Travel Distances (AD B Vol.2)
DirectionMax Distance
One direction only (dead-end)18m
Two or more directions45m

Measured from most remote point to nearest protected escape stair or final exit along the actual route of travel. Sprinkler provision allows increased distances in some configurations — refer to AD B Table 3 notes.

Evacuation Strategy
  • Simultaneous evacuation — standard for most offices. All persons evacuate on alarm.
  • Phased evacuation — used in large multi-floor office buildings where stair capacity may be an issue. Fire floor + floor above evacuate first, then others on second signal.
  • Fire Emergency Plan must nominate floor wardens, define assembly point, and cover out-of-hours procedures.
  • Fire drills — at least annually. RRFSO Art. 12 requires emergency procedures including drills; Art. 21 requires training. Frequency should be risk-based — annually as minimum.
Fire Detection (BS 5839-1)
System CategoryApplication
L3 / MSmall office — manual call points + detection on escape routes only
L2Medium/large office — detection throughout plus escape routes
L1High-risk or complex office — full automatic detection throughout

Phased evacuation buildings require two-stage alarm and voice alarm (PAVA) system — BS 5839-8.

Common FRA Findings
  • Fire doors to stairwells wedged open or self-closers removed
  • Compartmentation breached by service installations (IT cable runs, air conditioning)
  • Inadequate fire extinguisher provision or wrong type for risk (electrical hazards)
  • Emergency lighting inadequate in server rooms / basement car parks
  • No hot-work permit system for maintenance activities
  • Fire drill not conducted in last 12 months
🛍
Retail & Shops
Purpose Group 4 (Shop) — AD B Vol.2 / DCLG Offices & Shops Guide
RRFSO 2005 AD B Vol.2 DCLG Shops Guide
▼
Occupancy Calculation
Area TypeOccupancy Factor
Sales area (ground floor)2m² per person
Sales area (upper floors)3m² per person
Basement sales2m² per person
Storage / stock rooms30m² per person
Staff areas / offices6m² per person
Max occupancy = Net sales area (m²) ÷ 2 (ground floor)

Source: AD B Vol.2 Table B.1 / BS 9999 Table B.1

Travel Distances (AD B Vol.2)
DirectionMax Distance
One direction only18m
Two or more directions45m

Large shopping centres — refer to BS 9999 or fire engineering solution under PD 7974.

Evacuation Strategy
  • Simultaneous evacuation — standard for most retail premises
  • High public footfall — ensure clear signage, sufficient exits, and staff trained to assist customers
  • Crowd management considerations for large stores
  • Public address system for large shopping centres
🍽
Restaurants, Cafes & Food & Drink Premises
Assembly & Recreation Purpose Group — cooking risk, licensing, high occupancy
RRFSO 2005 Licensing Act 2003 AD B Vol.2
▼
Occupancy Calculation
AreaOccupancy Factor
Dining area (seated)1 per seat / 1m² per person standing
Bar / standing area0.5m² per person
Kitchen5m² per person (staff only)
Beer garden / externalCount separately — may need separate exits
Permitted occupancy = from premises licence condition (if licensed)

Licensing Act 2003 premises licence will state an occupancy limit — FRA must not exceed this. If no licence limit, use AD B calculation.

Travel Distances (AD B Vol.2 — Assembly)
DirectionMax Distance
One direction (seated area)15m
Two or more directions (seated)32m
Specific Risks & Findings
  • Cooking risk — deep fat fryers, gas ranges. Class F extinguisher or fixed suppression (Ansul/Amerex type) required for commercial fryers
  • Grease accumulation in extraction ductwork — major fire risk, ductwork cleaning records required
  • LPG cylinders — DSEAR assessment, storage and use compliance
  • Arson risk — external refuse storage secure and away from building
  • Emergency lighting in basement areas and escape routes
🏟
Stadia, Arenas & Large Assembly Venues
Special provisions — Guide to Safety at Sports Grounds (Green Guide), Safety at Sports Grounds Act
RRFSO 2005 Safety at Sports Grounds Act 1975 Green Guide 6th Ed
▼
Key Legislation & Guidance
  • Safety at Sports Grounds Act 1975 — designated sports grounds (10,000+ spectators) require a General Safety Certificate from the LA
  • Fire Safety and Safety of Places of Sport Act 1987 — regulated stands (500+ covered spectators)
  • Guide to Safety at Sports Grounds (Green Guide, 6th Ed.) — the primary technical reference for spectator safety including fire
  • RRFSO 2005 — fire risk assessment duty applies in addition
  • Licensing Act 2003 — premises licence for entertainment events
Capacity Calculation (Green Guide)

Holding capacity is the lower of:

  • Structural capacity — structural engineer's assessment
  • Entry/exit capacity — based on flow rates through entry/exit points
  • Emergency evacuation capacity — based on number and width of exits and time to evacuate
Exit capacity = (Total exit width in units of 550mm) × flow rate × evacuation time

Green Guide 6th Ed. specifies 8 minutes for uncovered areas, 2.5 minutes per tier for covered stands as general benchmark.

🏥
Hospitals & Acute Healthcare
Progressive Horizontal Evacuation, HTM 05 series, CQC registration
RRFSO 2005 HTM 05-01 / 05-02 / 05-03 BS 5839-1
▼
Key Guidance — HTM 05 Series (NHS England)
  • HTM 05-01: Managing healthcare fire safety — management duties, training, FRA
  • HTM 05-02: Guidance in support of functional provisions for healthcare premises — design and construction
  • HTM 05-03 Parts A–L: Component guidance — fire detection, suppression, doors, escape lighting, etc.
  • All NHS premises should comply with HTM 05 series as the sector-specific interpretation of RRFSO and Building Regs

Ref: NHS England HTM library (free)

Progressive Horizontal Evacuation (PHE)

PHE is the standard strategy for hospitals — patients cannot be evacuated simultaneously. Instead:

  • Stage 1: Immediate evacuation of fire compartment to adjacent compartment (same floor)
  • Stage 2: If fire cannot be controlled — move to further compartment (progressive)
  • Stage 3: Vertical evacuation to safe floor or ground if necessary

Requires minimum 2 compartments per floor, each capable of accommodating evacuated patients. Compartment walls = 60-min minimum fire resistance.

Compartment capacity = must accommodate all patients from adjacent compartment
Travel Distances (AD B / HTM 05-02)
DirectionDistance
One direction (sleeping risk)9m
Two directions (sleeping risk)18m (non-sprinklered) / 30m (sprinklered)

HTM 05-02 may specify shorter distances for wards with high-dependency patients.

Occupancy Considerations
  • Patient count = bed count (or occupancy registers for day wards)
  • Staff count from rota records
  • Visitor numbers — estimate from policy (often 2 per bed)
  • Fire emergency plan must account for non-ambulant patients — individual patient PEEP or ward GEEP
  • Patient mobility assessments critical — PEEP/GEEP process mandatory
👴
Care Homes & Residential Care
Sleeping risk, vulnerable occupants, CQC registered — PHE strategy typically adopted
RRFSO 2005 Health & Social Care Act 2008 FSRA Residential Care Guide
▼
Key Legislation
  • RRFSO 2005 — FRA duty; care homes are non-domestic premises
  • Health & Social Care Act 2008 / Care Act 2014 — CQC registration; fire safety is a fundamental standard (Reg. 15)
  • CQC Regulation 15 — premises must be safe including fire precautions
  • Res. Evac. Plans Regs 2025 — applies where residential accommodation provided
Evacuation Strategy
  • Progressive Horizontal Evacuation — standard approach for registered care homes with non-ambulant residents
  • PEEP required for every resident who cannot self-evacuate — updated at admission and when care needs change
  • Staff-to-resident ratio in Fire Emergency Plan must be sufficient to implement PHE
  • Night-time staffing levels must support evacuation — FRA must assess night risk specifically
  • Fire drills — at varying times including at least one at night, minimum 2 per year recommended (RRFSO Art. 12 emergency procedures; HTM 05-01 recommends more frequent for high-dependency wards)
Travel Distances
DirectionDistance
One direction (sleeping risk)9m
Two directions18m

Where PHE is adopted — distance to adjacent compartment (place of relative safety) not to final exit.

Detection (BS 5839-1)
  • Category L1 — full automatic detection throughout (required for registered care homes under FSRA guide)
  • Nurse call / wander alarm integration with fire alarm — check for compatibility
  • Deaf/hard of hearing residents — visual and/or vibrating alerting devices required
  • Quarterly checks by competent person — records kept
Common FRA Findings
  • Night staffing levels insufficient to implement PHE without delay
  • PEEPs out of date or not completed for all non-ambulant residents
  • Bedroom fire doors — self-closers removed by residents/staff for convenience
  • Smoking management — designated areas, resident supervision
  • Mattress/bedding fire resistance not assessed
  • Kitchen extraction ductwork cleaning overdue
♿
PCFRA — Person-Centred Fire Risk Assessment
PEEPs & GEEPs — ensuring individuals who cannot self-evacuate can do so safely. Applies across all premises with vulnerable or mobility-impaired occupants.
RRFSO Art. 9 & 18 Equality Act 2010 Res. Evac. Plans Regs 2025 BS 8300:2018 BS 9999 Annex F
▼
What is a PCFRA?

A Person-Centred Fire Risk Assessment (PCFRA) is a fire risk assessment methodology that places the individual — specifically those who cannot self-evacuate — at the centre of evacuation planning. It is the framework within which PEEPs and GEEPs are developed and managed.

A PCFRA supplements the standard RRFSO Art. 9 FRA — ensuring it fully accounts for every individual requiring evacuation assistance, with a documented plan for each.

Where PCFRAs are most critical:

  • Registered care homes and nursing homes
  • Hospitals and healthcare premises
  • Sheltered and supported housing
  • Schools and educational settings (SEN pupils)
  • Any workplace with disabled employees
  • Any premises open to the public
Legal Basis
  • RRFSO Art. 9 — FRA must identify groups at risk, including disabled persons
  • RRFSO Art. 18 — RP must nominate persons to assist evacuation
  • RRFSO Art. 11 — emergency procedures must account for all persons including those needing assistance
  • Equality Act 2010 — duty to make reasonable adjustments; failure to provide adequate PEEP may constitute disability discrimination
  • Res. Evac. Plans Regs 2025 — from 6 January 2025, RPs of multi-occupied residential buildings must have a documented process to offer PEEPs to residents needing assistance
  • Health & Social Care Act 2008 / CQC Reg. 15 — registered care providers must ensure safe evacuation for all residents
PEEP — Personal Emergency Evacuation Plan

A PEEP is an individually tailored written plan for a specific named person who cannot self-evacuate. Agreed with the individual, it sets out exactly how they will be assisted from any point in the building.

Who needs a PEEP: wheelchair users, mobility impairment (including temporary), visual impairment, hearing impairment, cognitive impairment or dementia, severe respiratory conditions, pregnancy where relevant, or any self-identifying individual.

PEEP = Person + Location(s) + Method + Named assistant(s) + Equipment + Communication + Review date

5-step PEEP process:

  1. Identify — HR records, self-disclosure, occupant register
  2. Assess — what can the person do? What assistance is needed? Which routes are accessible?
  3. Plan — document with the individual. Agree route, method, assistant, equipment
  4. Implement — train assistants, procure equipment, establish refuge if needed
  5. Review — when condition or premises change; at least annually

Equipment: Evacuation chairs (stairclimbers), vibrating pagers/BSL alerters for deaf/HoH, ski-pads for non-ambulant. All staff using equipment must be trained — records kept.

GEEP — Generic Emergency Evacuation Plan

A GEEP is a generic plan for assisting any disabled visitor or member of the public — where an individual PEEP cannot be prepared in advance because the person is not a known regular occupant.

GEEPs are essential for: public buildings, shops, offices, hotels, hospitals (outpatient/visitor areas), schools (visitor areas), any premises open to the public.

GEEP must include:

  • Staff trained to assist any disabled visitor
  • Refuge areas identified, marked and communicated to all staff
  • Evacuation chairs on each floor with trained operators
  • Two-way communication in refuges — BS 5839-9 EVCS linked to staffed control point
  • Procedure for FRS to locate persons in refuges
  • Regular testing of refuge communication systems and drills including GEEP scenarios
GEEP = Trained staff + Refuge areas + Equipment + Communication + FRS liaison
Key Standards & Guidance
  • BS 8300:2018 — accessible built environment; refuge design, evacuation lift provision
  • BS 9999:2017 Annex F — evacuation of people with disabilities
  • BS 9991:2015 Section 15 — disabled persons evacuation in residential buildings
  • BS 5839-9:2021 — emergency voice communication systems (refuge intercom)
  • PAS 79-2:2020 — FRA methodology including PEEP considerations for multi-occupied residential
  • HTM 05-01 — PCFRA and evacuation planning for healthcare
  • NFCC Guidance — Evacuation of disabled people from buildings
Common PCFRA Failings in FRAs
  • No PEEP process — reliance on verbal arrangements not documented
  • PEEPs not updated when individual's condition changes
  • PEEP not offered to all who may need one
  • Evacuation chairs present but no trained operators
  • Refuge areas not provided where non-simultaneous evacuation is adopted
  • No BS 5839-9 EVCS in refuges — persons cannot alert FRS
  • Post-January 2025 residential buildings — no documented PEEP offer process
  • Drills do not test PEEP/GEEP procedures
  • FRS not provided refuge locations in fire emergency plan
Refuges — Design & Requirements

A refuge is a place of relative safety — typically a protected lobby or stairwell enclosure — where a person awaiting assistance can wait, separated from fire by fire-resisting construction.

  • Minimum clear space: 1400mm × 1400mm (BS 8300) — sufficient for wheelchair plus companion
  • Two-way communication to staffed control point — BS 5839-9 EVCS mandatory in new buildings
  • Within protected fire-resisting enclosure
  • Signed — BS EN ISO 7010 E007 symbol
  • Location communicated to FRS and displayed within refuge
  • A refuge is not a place of ultimate safety — evacuation from it must still be completed
🏫
Schools, Colleges & Universities
DfE guidance, high occupancy, mixed-use buildings, arson risk
RRFSO 2005 DfE BB100 FSRA Educational Premises
▼
Key Guidance
  • DfE Building Bulletin BB100 — design for fire safety in schools (free from Gov.uk)
  • Gov.uk FSRA Educational Premises Guide — FRA process for schools under RRFSO
  • Protective Security and Resilience in Education (DfE) — includes fire security overlap
Occupancy Calculation
AreaOccupancy Factor
Classrooms1.5–2m² per person (or actual pupil numbers)
Assembly hall / sports hall0.5m² per person (standing) / 1 per seat
Corridors—
Offices / staff rooms6m² per person

For schools — use pupil roll + staff headcount as primary occupancy figure rather than floor area calculation.

Travel Distances (AD B Vol.2)
DirectionDistance
One direction18m
Two or more directions45m
School-Specific Risks
  • Arson — highest single cause of school fires. Perimeter security, out-of-hours detection, refuse management critical
  • Science labs — chemical storage, DSEAR assessment required for flammable substances
  • DT workshops — dust extraction, machinery fire risks
  • After-hours lettings — separate FRA assessment; responsibility for lettings users
  • SEN pupils — individual evacuation plans; PEEP process
  • Modular / temporary classrooms — compartmentation, detection, means of escape
🏨
Hotels, Hostels & Bed & Breakfasts
Sleeping risk — guests unfamiliar with premises, short-term stay
RRFSO 2005 FSRA Sleeping Accommodation BS 5839-1
▼
Occupancy

Use maximum bedspace capacity as occupancy figure — not actual occupancy at time of FRA.

AreaFactor
BedroomsMax bedspace count
Restaurant/bar1m² per person / seat count
Conference rooms0.5–1m² per person
Travel Distances (AD B Vol.2)
DirectionDistance
Dead-end bedroom corridor9m
Two directions from bedroom18m

Measured from bedroom door to protected stair. Inner rooms (rooms only accessible through another room) not permitted for sleeping accommodation.

Detection (BS 5839-1)
  • Category L2 minimum — detection throughout escape routes and in each bedroom
  • L1 recommended for large hotels — full detection throughout
  • Alarm must be audible in all sleeping areas — minimum 75dB(A) at bedhead (BS 5839-1)
  • Guests unfamiliar with premises — clear escape route signage essential
Evacuation Strategy
  • Simultaneous evacuation — standard for most hotels and B&Bs
  • Fire Emergency Plan must consider guests who are unfamiliar with layout — floor plans in rooms, clear signage
  • Night porter / night manager role in raising alarm and assisting evacuation must be documented
  • PEEPs for mobility-impaired guests — booking process should identify need
🏭
Factories, Warehouses & Industrial
Purpose Group 6 (Industrial) / Group 7 (Storage) — process risk, high fire load
RRFSO 2005 DSEAR 2002 AD B Vol.2
▼
Occupancy Calculation
Area TypeOccupancy Factor
Factory / production area5m² per person (light) / 10–30m² (heavy industrial)
Warehouse (storage)30m² per person
Offices within factory6m² per person

Use actual workforce headcount (maximum shift) as primary figure — floor area method may undercount in automated facilities.

Travel Distances (AD B Vol.2)
DirectionDistance
One direction25m (factory) / 25m (storage)
Two or more directions45m

Higher-hazard processes (flammable liquids, dusts) — reduce travel distances. DSEAR ATEX zoning must be considered.

DSEAR Interaction
  • Where flammable substances are used or stored — DSEAR 2002 risk assessment required alongside FRA
  • ATEX zones must be classified (Zone 0, 1, 2 for gases/vapours; Zone 20, 21, 22 for dusts)
  • Ignition sources must be controlled within ATEX zones — includes hot work, electrical equipment
  • FRA and DSEAR assessment must be co-ordinated — same RP in many cases
🏛
Heritage & Listed Buildings
Balancing fire safety with conservation — PD 7974, BS 7913, Historic England guidance
RRFSO 2005 Planning (Listed Buildings) Act 1990 PD 7974 Historic England Guidance
▼
Key Considerations
  • RRFSO Art. 9 FRA duty applies — fire safety obligations cannot be waived for listed buildings
  • Listed Building Consent required for any alterations affecting character — fire safety works must be approved by LPA
  • Early engagement with LPA Conservation Officer and FRS recommended
  • Standard prescriptive solutions (AD B) may not be achievable without harm to fabric — fire engineering approach (PD 7974) often required
Key Guidance
  • Historic England — Fire Safety in Historic Buildings (free guidance)
  • BS 7913:2022 — Guide to the conservation of historic buildings (includes fire risk)
  • PD 7974 — fire safety engineering framework for non-standard solutions
  • ICOMOS — Principles for Fire Safety in Heritage Buildings

Ref: Historic England Fire Safety guidance (free)

Common Heritage-Specific Risks
  • Timber frame and lath-and-plaster construction — rapid fire spread within voids
  • Thatched roofs — highly combustible, chimney proximity
  • Open timber roofs / atria — large undivided volumes
  • Original features that cannot be altered — may preclude standard compartmentation
  • Void spaces — difficulty in achieving fire stopping
  • Electrical rewiring history unknown — periodic inspection (EICR) essential
📋
Pre-Construction Fire Risk Assessment (Pre-Construction FRA)
Fire risk during construction, refurbishment, and demolition works
RRFSO 2005 CDM Regs 2015 HSE HSG168
▼
Key Legislation
  • RRFSO 2005 — applies to construction sites and existing buildings where works are carried out. RP = principal contractor typically
  • CDM Regulations 2015 — principal designer and principal contractor must consider fire safety in pre-construction phase
  • Health & Safety at Work Act 1974 / Construction (Design and Management) Regulations 2015
  • RRFSO Art. 22 — co-operation between contractors and building occupiers where both present
Pre-Construction FRA — Contents & Methodology

A Pre-Construction FRA should address:

  • Ignition sources — hot works (cutting, welding, grinding), electrical installations, temporary heating
  • Fuel sources — construction materials, waste, flammable liquids, temporary structures
  • Spread of fire — void spaces, ductwork, partial compartmentation during works
  • Detection & warning — temporary fire alarm, liaison with existing building systems
  • Means of escape — construction routes, hoarding, temporary stair access
  • Management — hot work permits, waste management, smoking policy, site induction
Hot Work Permit system — mandatory for any cutting, welding, grinding, or use of naked flames
Key Guidance
  • HSE HSG168 — Fire Safety in Construction (free from HSE)
  • CFOA/NFCC — Fire Safety on Construction Sites guidance
  • JCoP — Joint Code of Practice on the Protection from Fire of Construction Sites and Buildings Undergoing Renovation (insurers)
ASFP Standards Index
Search the Association for Specialist Fire Protection standards index — covering all BS, BS EN, and international standards relevant to passive fire protection, fire stopping, fire doors, ductwork, and structural fire protection. Data sourced from asfpindex.org.uk.

Voice-to-write-up tool. Record your site observations, get the complete Additional Information section (pages 7–11) written in your voice, ready to paste into your 4site report template.
